scenarios.title: Routine referral: suspected obstructive sleep apnoea
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Thomas Holm  
**DOB:** 14/03/1971 (aged 55)  
**Occupation:** Sales manager  
**Marital status:** Married, two adult children  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026

**Reason for today's visit:**  
Follow-up of daytime sleepiness and witnessed apnoeic episodes reported by wife.

**Subjective:**
- 6/12 history of excessive daytime sleepiness; falls asleep during meetings.
- Two near-miss incidents at the wheel in the last 8 weeks.
- Wife reports loud snoring with apnoeic pauses, ending in choking gasps.
- Morning headaches (3-4 mornings per week), unrefreshing sleep.
- Denies chest pain, palpitations, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea.
- Mood low but no suicidal ideation.

**Objective:**
- BP 142/88 mmHg (right arm, seated, after 5 min rest)
- Pulse 78, regular
- SpO2 96% on room air
- BMI 32 (height 1.78 m, weight 101 kg)
- Neck circumference 44 cm
- Mallampati class III
- ENT: no obvious upper airway abnormality
- Cardiovascular and respiratory exam unremarkable

**Validated tools:**
- Epworth Sleepiness Scale: 18 (severe)
- STOP-BANG: 6/8 (high risk)

**Past medical history:**
- Hypertension (since 2022), on amlodipine 10 mg daily
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (since 2023), on metformin 500 mg BD; HbA1c 56 mmol/mol (Mar 2026)
- Hypercholesterolaemia, on atorvastatin 20 mg nocte

**Social history:**
- Ex-smoker (stopped 2019; 20 pack-year history)
- Alcohol 8 units/week
- No recreational drug use

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Plan from today:**
- Refer to sleep clinic for overnight polysomnography
- Advise DVLA notification due to driving risk
- Continue current antihypertensive and statin therapy
- Review in 6 weeks or sooner if symptoms worsen

**Writing task:** Using the case notes, write a routine referral letter to Dr Helena Park, Consultant in Sleep Medicine, City General Hospital, requesting assessment and management. Do NOT use bullet points or note form. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Thomas Holm, aged 55, sales manager, married with two adult children.
Follow-up of daytime sleepiness and witnessed apnoeic episodes reported by wife.
Six-month history of excessive daytime sleepiness; falls asleep during meetings.
Two near-miss incidents at the wheel in the last eight weeks.
Wife reports loud snoring with apnoeic pauses ending in choking gasps.
Morning headaches three to four mornings per week, unrefreshing sleep.
Denies chest pain, palpitations, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea.
Mood low but no suicidal ideation.
Blood pressure 142/88 mmHg, pulse 78 regular, oxygen saturation 96% on room air.
BMI 32, neck circumference 44 cm, Mallampati class III.
ENT, cardiovascular and respiratory examinations otherwise unremarkable.
Epworth Sleepiness Scale 18 (severe); STOP-BANG 6 of 8 (high risk).
Past history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypercholesterolaemia.
Current medications include amlodipine 10 mg daily, metformin 500 mg twice daily and atorvastatin 20 mg nightly.
Ex-smoker since 2019 with a 20 pack-year history; alcohol 8 units per week.
Referred to sleep clinic for overnight polysomnography; DVLA notification advised.

scenarios.title: Urgent referral: suspected acute coronary syndrome
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Raj Patel  
**DOB:** 09/11/1978 (aged 47)  
**Occupation:** Taxi driver  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026, 14:20 hrs

**Reason for today's visit:**  
Attended walk-in surgery with 90-minute history of central chest pain.

**Subjective:**
- Sudden onset crushing central chest pain at rest 90 minutes ago.
- Radiates to left arm and jaw; described as 8/10 in severity.
- Associated nausea, sweating, and shortness of breath.
- No relief with rest; no glyceryl trinitrate available.
- Denies recent trauma, recent flights, lower limb swelling.
- No previous cardiac history; no chest pain prior to today.

**Objective:**
- Distressed, diaphoretic, peripheral pallor
- BP 148/92 right arm; 146/90 left arm
- Pulse 102, regular
- Respiratory rate 22
- SpO2 95% on room air
- Temperature 36.8 degrees Celsius
- Heart sounds normal; no murmurs; no added sounds
- Chest: equal air entry; no crackles or wheeze
- Abdomen soft and non-tender
- 12-lead ECG: 1 mm ST elevation in leads II, III and aVF; reciprocal depression in I and aVL

**Past medical history:**
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus, on metformin 500 mg BD; last HbA1c 64 mmol/mol
- Hypertension, on ramipril 5 mg daily
- Hyperlipidaemia, on simvastatin 40 mg nocte

**Family history:** Father had myocardial infarction at age 52.

**Social history:**
- Current smoker, 15 cigarettes per day
- Alcohol 4 units per week

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action taken at surgery:**
- Aspirin 300 mg PO chewed
- Sublingual GTN spray (one dose), partial relief
- 999 ambulance called; ETA 8 minutes
- IV access secured

**Writing task:** Write an urgent referral letter to the on-call cardiology team at Royal Northern Hospital A&E, requesting immediate assessment. Use letter prose, not bullet points. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Raj Patel, aged 47, taxi driver, attended walk-in surgery 90 minutes ago.
Sudden onset crushing central chest pain at rest, radiating to left arm and jaw.
Severity 8 out of 10, with associated nausea, diaphoresis and shortness of breath.
Pain not relieved with rest; no glyceryl trinitrate available.
Denies recent trauma, recent flights or lower limb swelling.
No previous cardiac history; no chest pain prior to today.
Appears distressed, diaphoretic with peripheral pallor.
Blood pressure 148/92 right arm, 146/90 left arm; pulse 102 regular; SpO2 95 percent on room air.
ECG showed 1 mm ST elevation in leads II, III and aVF with reciprocal depression in I and aVL.
Past history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension and hyperlipidaemia.
Current medications include metformin, ramipril and simvastatin.
Father had a myocardial infarction at age 52.
Current smoker of 15 cigarettes per day; alcohol 4 units weekly.
Aspirin 300 mg given orally, sublingual GTN spray administered with partial relief.
Ambulance called with estimated arrival of 8 minutes; intravenous access secured.

scenarios.title: Discharge to GP: community-acquired pneumonia
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Eleanor Carter  
**DOB:** 22/07/1963 (aged 62)  
**Admission date:** 19/05/2026  
**Discharge date:** 26/05/2026 (today)

**Reason for admission:**  
Community-acquired pneumonia (CURB-65 score 2 on admission).

**Presenting complaint on admission:**
- 4-day history of productive cough with greenish sputum
- Pleuritic right-sided chest pain
- Fever, rigors and reduced oral intake

**Investigations on admission:**
- Chest X-ray: right lower zone consolidation
- WCC 16.2, CRP 198, urea 7.8, creatinine 92
- Blood cultures: Streptococcus pneumoniae sensitive to amoxicillin
- Sputum culture: same organism
- COVID-19 swab: negative

**Inpatient management:**
- IV co-amoxiclav 1.2 g TDS for 48 hours, then oral amoxicillin 500 mg TDS
- Paracetamol 1 g QDS PRN
- IV fluids on day 1 only
- Oxygen via nasal cannulae 2 L/min for 24 hours
- Daily physiotherapy review for chest clearance

**Progress:**
- Apyrexial from day 3 onwards
- Oxygen weaned by day 4
- WCC 8.2, CRP 22 on day 7
- Tolerating diet and mobilising independently

**At discharge:**
- Afebrile, observations stable, ambulating without aid
- Oxygen saturations 97% on room air
- Discharge medications: oral amoxicillin 500 mg TDS to complete 7-day course
- No new social concerns identified

**Past medical history:**
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus, on metformin 1 g BD
- Hypertension, on ramipril 10 mg daily
- Mild chronic kidney disease (eGFR 56)

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Follow-up plan:**
- Repeat chest X-ray in 6 weeks to confirm resolution
- GP to monitor renal function in 2 weeks given recent IV antibiotics
- Patient education on pneumococcal vaccination provided

**Writing task:** Write a discharge letter to Mrs Carter's GP, Dr Imran Khan at The Meadows Surgery, requesting ongoing care. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mrs Eleanor Carter was admitted on 19 May 2026 with community-acquired pneumonia.
She presented with a four-day history of productive cough with greenish sputum.
Other features included pleuritic right-sided chest pain, fever, rigors and reduced oral intake.
Chest X-ray demonstrated right lower zone consolidation.
Blood cultures grew Streptococcus pneumoniae sensitive to amoxicillin.
She received intravenous co-amoxiclav for 48 hours, followed by oral amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily.
She became apyrexial from day three and was weaned off oxygen by day four.
Her white cell count fell to 8.2 and CRP to 22 by day seven.
On discharge today she is afebrile, ambulating independently with oxygen saturations of 97 percent on room air.
She is discharged on oral amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily to complete a seven-day course.
Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension and mild chronic kidney disease.
She remains on metformin 1 g twice daily and ramipril 10 mg daily.
A repeat chest X-ray is requested in six weeks to confirm radiological resolution.
The GP is asked to monitor renal function in two weeks following the recent intravenous antibiotics.
Patient education on pneumococcal vaccination has been provided during the admission.

scenarios.title: Transfer to specialist unit: decompensated heart failure
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Frederick Mason  
**DOB:** 02/01/1948 (aged 78)  
**Current location:** Ward 6, District General Hospital  
**Transfer date:** 26/05/2026 (today)

**Reason for transfer:**  
Decompensated heart failure with significant fluid overload, refractory to ward-based diuresis. Transfer to Cardiology Specialist Unit at Royal Northern Hospital for advanced management and consideration of haemofiltration.

**Admission details:**
- Admitted 22/05/2026 with acute breathlessness and bilateral leg swelling
- Initial weight 96 kg (baseline weight 78 kg)
- BNP 4250 ng/L
- Echocardiogram: severely impaired left ventricular systolic function, ejection fraction 18%
- Chest X-ray: bilateral pleural effusions, pulmonary oedema

**Inpatient course:**
- Day 1: IV furosemide 80 mg BD; produced 1.5 L urine over 24 hours
- Day 2-3: Furosemide infusion at 10 mg/hr; output remained <1 L/day
- Day 3: Renal function deteriorating (creatinine rose from 110 to 168)
- Day 4: Hyponatraemia (Na 128); fluid restricted to 1 L/day
- Today: weight 92 kg (only 4 kg loss over 4 days); persistent peripheral and pulmonary oedema; orthopnoea remains marked

**Current observations:**
- BP 96/62, pulse 102 regular
- RR 26, SpO2 92% on 4 L/min oxygen
- JVP elevated at 8 cm above sternal angle
- Bilateral basal crackles
- Pitting oedema to mid-thigh bilaterally

**Past medical history:**
- Ischaemic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (since 2018)
- Atrial fibrillation, on apixaban 5 mg BD
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus, on insulin glargine 30 units nocte
- Chronic kidney disease stage 3b (baseline creatinine 130)

**Current medications:**
- Bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily
- Ramipril 5 mg daily (held since admission due to AKI)
- Spironolactone 25 mg daily
- Apixaban 5 mg BD
- Insulin glargine 30 units nocte

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Writing task:** Write a transfer letter to Dr Niamh O'Sullivan, Consultant Cardiologist, Royal Northern Hospital, summarising the admission and requesting acceptance for specialist management. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Frederick Mason, aged 78, is being transferred from Ward 6 to the Cardiology Specialist Unit.
He has decompensated heart failure with significant fluid overload that has proved refractory to ward-based diuresis.
He was admitted on 22 May 2026 with acute breathlessness and bilateral leg swelling.
Admission weight was 96 kg against a baseline of 78 kg and BNP was 4250 ng/L.
Echocardiography showed severely impaired left ventricular systolic function with an ejection fraction of 18 percent.
Initial intravenous furosemide produced only 1.5 L of urine over 24 hours.
Furosemide infusion at 10 mg per hour failed to achieve adequate diuresis.
Renal function deteriorated with creatinine rising from 110 to 168.
Hyponatraemia developed with sodium of 128 and fluid restriction was introduced.
Despite four days of treatment, weight loss has been only 4 kg and orthopnoea remains marked.
Current observations show blood pressure 96/62, pulse 102, oxygen saturations 92 percent on 4 L per minute oxygen.
Past medical history includes ischaemic heart failure, atrial fibrillation, type 2 diabetes mellitus and chronic kidney disease stage 3b.
He remains on bisoprolol, spironolactone, apixaban and insulin glargine.
Ramipril has been held since admission due to acute kidney injury.

scenarios.title: Counselling letter: anticoagulant initiation
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Bernadette Ahmed  
**DOB:** 18/06/1955 (aged 70)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Community pharmacy, first prescription collected.

**Reason for letter:**  
New prescription for warfarin following recent diagnosis of atrial fibrillation. Patient attended for first dispense and counselling at the pharmacy. Writing to inform the GP of counselling provided and outstanding monitoring requirements.

**New medication issued today:**
- Warfarin 1 mg and 3 mg tablets, doses to be titrated against INR per yellow book
- Initial loading regimen as prescribed: 5 mg day 1, 5 mg day 2, dose adjustment from day 3 based on INR
- Yellow OAC card and warfarin information booklet issued

**Counselling provided in pharmacy (30 minutes):**
- Dose timing: take at the same time each day, evening recommended
- Importance of consistent dietary vitamin K intake (green leafy vegetables)
- Need for regular INR monitoring (initially every 3-4 days, then weekly, then monthly)
- Recognition of bleeding signs: prolonged epistaxis, haematuria, melaena, unusual bruising
- Risks of over-the-counter medications: avoid aspirin and ibuprofen unless cleared by prescriber
- Importance of informing all healthcare providers (dentist, pharmacy, A&E) of warfarin use
- Demonstrated correct use of yellow OAC monitoring book

**Concerns identified:**
- Mrs Ahmed already purchases ibuprofen 200 mg over the counter for chronic knee pain (3-4 tablets daily). Advised to discontinue and seek GP review for alternative analgesia.
- She is unsure how to take her existing levothyroxine alongside the new warfarin; reassured that timing does not interact.
- Mild visual impairment noted; tablets read with magnifier. Considered carer assistance with dosing book entries.

**Current medications:**
- Levothyroxine 75 micrograms once daily
- Atenolol 50 mg once daily
- Ibuprofen 200 mg PRN (purchased OTC, advised to stop)
- Adcal-D3 two tablets daily

**Past medical history:**
- Atrial fibrillation (newly diagnosed, 18/05/2026)
- Primary hypothyroidism
- Hypertension
- Osteoarthritis (right knee)

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action requested from GP:**
- Review of analgesia plan to replace ibuprofen with a warfarin-compatible alternative
- Confirmation of INR monitoring arrangements (anticoagulation clinic vs practice nurse)
- Consideration of carer support for medicines management

**Writing task:** Write a letter to Dr Helena Park at Riverside Medical Centre about the counselling delivered and the outstanding actions required. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mrs Bernadette Ahmed, aged 70, collected her first warfarin prescription at the pharmacy today.
Warfarin was initiated following a recent diagnosis of atrial fibrillation on 18 May 2026.
She received 30 minutes of structured counselling at the pharmacy.
Counselling covered dosing, dietary vitamin K, INR monitoring, bleeding signs and over-the-counter medication risks.
A yellow oral anticoagulant card and warfarin information booklet were issued.
Mrs Ahmed currently purchases ibuprofen 200 mg over the counter for chronic knee pain.
She was advised to discontinue ibuprofen and to seek GP review for alternative analgesia.
She was reassured that the timing of her existing levothyroxine does not interact with warfarin.
Mild visual impairment was noted; carer assistance with dosing book entries was discussed.
Current medications include levothyroxine 75 micrograms daily, atenolol 50 mg daily and Adcal-D3 two tablets daily.
Past medical history includes newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation, primary hypothyroidism, hypertension and right knee osteoarthritis.
The GP is asked to review her analgesia plan and select a warfarin-compatible alternative.
Confirmation of INR monitoring arrangements is requested.
Consideration of carer support for medicines management is suggested.

scenarios.title: Drug-interaction warning: warfarin plus NSAID
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Geoffrey Williams  
**DOB:** 03/02/1949 (aged 77)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Community pharmacy clinical-check intervention.

**Reason for letter:**  
To alert prescriber to a high-risk co-prescription of warfarin and a newly added oral NSAID (naproxen 500 mg BD) and to request review and substitution.

**Prescription details flagged:**
- Repeat prescription presented today included warfarin 3 mg daily AND newly added naproxen 500 mg BD with proton pump inhibitor cover
- Pharmacy clinical-check identified clinically significant interaction (bleeding risk, particularly upper GI haemorrhage)
- Last INR (per yellow book): 2.6, target 2-3 (on 18/05/2026)

**Patient interview today:**
- Confirms knee pain has worsened; prior paracetamol 1 g QDS giving inadequate relief
- Has been taking the naproxen as prescribed since prescription issued 5 days ago
- Reports two small episodes of gum bleeding while brushing teeth in the past 48 hours
- No haematemesis, melaena or haematuria
- No epigastric pain or dyspepsia
- Current INR not yet rechecked since naproxen started

**Action taken at pharmacy today:**
- Withheld naproxen supply pending prescriber review (patient understands and consents)
- Supplied paracetamol 1 g QDS as interim cover
- Advised to attend immediate medical review if any bleeding worsens
- Booked priority INR check for 27/05/2026
- Provided written explanation of the interaction for patient

**Current medications (all repeat):**
- Warfarin 3 mg daily
- Bisoprolol 5 mg daily
- Atorvastatin 40 mg nocte
- Lansoprazole 30 mg daily
- Paracetamol 1 g QDS PRN
- Naproxen 500 mg BD (NEW; withheld today)

**Past medical history:**
- Atrial fibrillation (warfarin since 2018)
- Hypertension
- Hypercholesterolaemia
- Right knee osteoarthritis

**Allergies:** Codeine (rash).

**Action requested from prescriber:**
- Discontinue naproxen and select an alternative analgesic appropriate to anticoagulated patients (e.g., topical NSAID, paracetamol with weak opioid, or referral for intra-articular intervention)
- Confirm whether INR rechecked

**Writing task:** Write a letter to Dr Akinwale Adeyemi, Mr Williams' usual GP, about the interaction identified and the action taken at the pharmacy. Use letter prose, not bullet points. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Geoffrey Williams, aged 77, presented his repeat prescription at the pharmacy today.
The prescription included warfarin 3 mg daily alongside newly added naproxen 500 mg twice daily.
A clinically significant interaction was identified at clinical check, with elevated bleeding risk.
His most recent INR was 2.6 on 18 May 2026, within the target range of 2 to 3.
He has been taking the naproxen as prescribed for the past five days.
He has experienced two small episodes of gum bleeding when brushing teeth in the past 48 hours.
He denies haematemesis, melaena, haematuria or epigastric pain.
The naproxen supply has been withheld pending prescriber review, with the patient's informed consent.
Paracetamol 1 g four times daily has been supplied as interim cover.
A priority INR check has been booked for 27 May 2026.
He has been advised to seek immediate medical review if bleeding worsens.
Other current medications include bisoprolol 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly and lansoprazole 30 mg daily.
Past medical history includes atrial fibrillation on warfarin since 2018, hypertension, hypercholesterolaemia and right knee osteoarthritis.
He has a documented allergy to codeine, which caused a rash.
The prescriber is asked to discontinue naproxen and select an analgesic alternative compatible with anticoagulation.

scenarios.title: Communication to GP: monitored dosage system adherence query
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Doris Lyle  
**DOB:** 12/09/1939 (aged 86)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Pharmacy medicines-use review.

**Reason for letter:**  
Concerns about Mrs Lyle's adherence with her monitored dosage system (MDS), noted by community pharmacy team during weekly delivery service.

**Subjective:**
- Mrs Lyle lives alone in a sheltered housing flat
- Has been on a weekly MDS (blister pack) supplied by this pharmacy since 2023
- Family member raised concern that her tablets are not being taken consistently
- Mrs Lyle herself reports occasional confusion about whether morning or evening dose was taken
- No formal cognitive assessment recorded on patient summary

**Pharmacy team observations over past 4 weeks:**
- Returned MDS from last week contained 6 unused doses out of 28 (21% missed)
- Two weeks ago: 4 unused doses; week before that: 3 unused doses
- Mrs Lyle has occasionally telephoned the pharmacy uncertain whether she has taken her morning dose
- She reports falls in the past 2 months (two falls, no fracture); attended A&E once

**Medicines included in MDS:**
- Donepezil 10 mg nocte (commenced 2023 for cognitive decline)
- Apixaban 2.5 mg BD (atrial fibrillation, dose-reduced for age and renal function)
- Bisoprolol 1.25 mg daily
- Lansoprazole 30 mg daily
- Senna 7.5 mg nocte

**Past medical history:**
- Cognitive decline (donepezil since 2023)
- Atrial fibrillation
- Hypertension
- Recurrent falls (per A&E discharge note)
- Mild chronic kidney disease (eGFR 42)

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action taken at pharmacy:**
- Conducted brief structured medicines-use review
- Reinforced day-of-week labelling on the MDS
- Considered switch to a multi-compartment pre-filled system with day/time labels enlarged
- Discussed possibility of carer prompt at morning and evening doses

**Action requested from GP:**
- Formal cognitive assessment to inform safe medicines management
- Review of falls risk in context of donepezil and apixaban
- Referral consideration to community pharmacist domiciliary medication review service
- Liaison with Mrs Lyle's family member for shared care planning

**Writing task:** Write a letter to Mrs Lyle's GP, Dr Helena Park at Riverside Medical Centre, about the adherence concerns identified and actions requested. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mrs Doris Lyle, aged 86, lives alone in sheltered housing and has used a weekly monitored dosage system since 2023.
Concerns have been raised about her adherence by both her family and the pharmacy delivery team.
She reports occasional confusion about whether morning or evening doses have been taken.
Returned blister packs over the past four weeks have contained 3 to 6 unused doses per week, with 21 percent missed last week.
She has telephoned the pharmacy on several occasions uncertain whether she has taken her morning dose.
She has had two falls in the past two months with one attendance at A&E and no fracture.
Medicines in the dosage system include donepezil, apixaban, bisoprolol, lansoprazole and senna.
Past medical history includes cognitive decline, atrial fibrillation, hypertension, recurrent falls and mild chronic kidney disease.
A brief structured medicines-use review was conducted today.
Day-of-week labelling on the MDS has been reinforced and enlarged time labels are being considered.
A formal cognitive assessment is requested to inform safe medicines management.
A falls risk review in the context of donepezil and apixaban is suggested.
Referral for community pharmacist domiciliary medication review is recommended.
Liaison with her family member for shared care planning is suggested.

scenarios.title: Response to GP: insulin titration query
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Daniel Okonkwo  
**DOB:** 25/04/1965 (aged 61)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Pharmacist-led diabetes clinic, hospital outpatients.

**Reason for letter:**  
Response to Dr Sarah Ahmed (GP) regarding her enquiry of 19/05/2026 about Mr Okonkwo's insulin titration and ongoing glycaemic control. He attended the pharmacist-led diabetes clinic today.

**Background from GP letter (received 19/05/2026):**
- HbA1c 78 mmol/mol despite uptitration of metformin and addition of dapagliflozin
- Capillary glucose readings consistently 11-14 mmol/L pre-breakfast
- GP requested pharmacy clinic to commence basal insulin titration and provide structured patient counselling

**Today's clinic findings:**
- Mr Okonkwo's home glucose diary (4-week record) reviewed in clinic
- Fasting glucose 10-13 mmol/L; pre-evening meal 9-11 mmol/L
- No nocturnal hypoglycaemia reported
- BMI 31; weight stable over past 12 months
- Renal function: eGFR 68; creatinine 110
- Liver function tests normal
- No diabetic ketoacidosis history

**Action taken in clinic:**
- Initiated insulin glargine 12 units subcutaneously at bedtime
- Demonstrated injection technique using pre-filled pen device
- Issued sharps bin and disposal advice
- Provided structured 'safe insulin start' counselling pack
- Reinforced sick-day rules and hypoglycaemia recognition
- Booked weekly telephone titration reviews for the next 6 weeks
- Suggested he continue current oral antihyperglycaemics (metformin 1 g BD, dapagliflozin 10 mg daily)

**Titration plan agreed:**
- Titrate up by 2 units every 3 days if fasting glucose remains above 7 mmol/L
- Titrate down by 2 units if fasting glucose falls below 5 mmol/L
- Target fasting glucose 5-7 mmol/L

**Past medical history:**
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (since 2015)
- Hypertension, on amlodipine 5 mg daily
- Diabetic retinopathy (background, last screened 2026)

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action requested from GP:**
- Repeat HbA1c in 12 weeks
- Annual nephropathy screening (UACR)
- Continued joint care; pharmacy clinic will provide weekly titration support for next 6 weeks then handover

**Writing task:** Write a reply letter to Dr Sarah Ahmed at Riverside Medical Centre summarising today's pharmacist-led clinic and the agreed plan. Use letter prose. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Daniel Okonkwo, aged 61, attended the pharmacist-led diabetes clinic today in response to Dr Sarah Ahmed's letter of 19 May 2026.
His most recent HbA1c was 78 mmol/mol despite metformin and dapagliflozin.
His fasting glucose readings have been consistently 10 to 13 mmol/L.
He reports no nocturnal hypoglycaemia.
Renal function remains acceptable with an eGFR of 68 and creatinine of 110.
Insulin glargine 12 units has been initiated at bedtime subcutaneously.
Injection technique has been demonstrated using a pre-filled pen device.
He has received a structured safe insulin start counselling pack and sick-day rules education.
Weekly telephone titration reviews have been booked for the next six weeks.
He will continue metformin 1 g twice daily and dapagliflozin 10 mg daily.
The titration plan is to increase by 2 units every three days if fasting glucose remains above 7 mmol/L.
Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes since 2015, hypertension and background diabetic retinopathy.
BMI is 31 and weight has been stable over the past 12 months.
Repeat HbA1c in 12 weeks and annual nephropathy screening are requested from the GP.
Joint care will be continued and pharmacy will hand over after the 6-week titration period.

scenarios.title: Discharge home: post-operative knee replacement
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Sandra Bell  
**DOB:** 30/10/1957 (aged 68)  
**Admission date:** 22/05/2026  
**Discharge date:** 26/05/2026 (today)

**Reason for admission:**  
Elective right total knee replacement.

**Operative details:**
- Right total knee replacement performed 23/05/2026 by Mr P Chowdhury (consultant orthopaedic surgeon)
- Spinal anaesthesia, no intra-operative complications
- Wound closed with absorbable sutures and Opsite dressing

**Inpatient progress:**
- Day 1 post-op: mobilised with physiotherapist to chair using Zimmer frame
- Day 2: stepped down to elbow crutches, ambulated 10 metres on ward
- Day 3: independent transfers, stairs assessment passed
- Pain controlled with paracetamol 1 g QDS regular and oramorph 5 mg PRN (used twice on day 1, not since)
- Wound dry, no signs of infection; dressing changed on day 3
- Bowels opened day 2 post-op
- Independent with personal care from day 3

**At discharge:**
- Apyrexial; observations stable
- Wound clean and dry; dressing intact and to remain for 10 days from surgery date
- Pain controlled with paracetamol 1 g QDS plus codeine 30 mg QDS PRN
- Mobile with elbow crutches; weight-bearing as tolerated
- Knee flexion 85 degrees; extension full
- Patient and husband confident with home exercises

**Past medical history:**
- Hypertension, on amlodipine 5 mg daily
- Osteoarthritis (multiple joints)
- No known cognitive impairment

**Allergies:** Penicillin (rash).

**Discharge medications:**
- Paracetamol 1 g QDS regular for 6 weeks
- Codeine phosphate 30 mg QDS PRN
- Amlodipine 5 mg daily (continued)
- Tinzaparin 4500 units SC daily for 14 days from surgery (VTE prophylaxis)
- Lactulose 15 mL BD PRN constipation

**Home circumstances:**
- Lives with husband (fit and well) in bungalow
- Toilet and shower on ground floor
- One step at front door, handrail in place

**Required home care:**
- District nurse referral for daily tinzaparin SC injections for 14 days (husband unable to administer due to needle phobia)
- District nurse to check wound on day 7 post-op and remove dressing day 10
- Community physiotherapy referral for 6-week home exercise programme
- Follow-up appointment booked: orthopaedic clinic 6 weeks post-op

**Writing task:** Write a discharge letter to the community nursing team at North Riverside Community Hub asking them to take over daily tinzaparin administration and wound checks. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mrs Sandra Bell was admitted on 22 May 2026 for an elective right total knee replacement.
Surgery was performed on 23 May 2026 by Mr P Chowdhury under spinal anaesthesia with no intra-operative complications.
She mobilised with physiotherapy from day 1 post-op and progressed to independent transfers by day 3.
Pain has been well controlled with paracetamol 1 g four times daily and PRN oramorph initially.
Her wound is dry with no signs of infection and the dressing is intact.
She is discharged on paracetamol regular, codeine PRN, amlodipine, tinzaparin and PRN lactulose.
She has been prescribed tinzaparin 4500 units subcutaneously daily for 14 days from surgery date.
Her husband is unable to administer injections due to needle phobia.
The district nurse is requested to administer the daily tinzaparin injections for the remaining 10 days.
The district nurse is also asked to check the wound on day 7 post-op and remove the dressing on day 10.
Past medical history includes hypertension and osteoarthritis of multiple joints.
She has a documented allergy to penicillin, which caused a rash.
She lives with her husband in a bungalow with a ground-floor toilet and shower.
Community physiotherapy referral has been arranged for a 6-week home exercise programme.
An orthopaedic clinic appointment has been booked for 6 weeks post-operatively.

scenarios.title: Transfer to community team: post-discharge wound care
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Henry Adetola  
**DOB:** 11/04/1948 (aged 78)  
**Transfer date:** 26/05/2026 (today)  
**Setting:** Acute trust tissue viability service, handover to community nursing.

**Reason for transfer:**  
Handover of complex venous leg ulcer dressing regime from acute tissue viability service to community nursing team for continued management at home.

**Wound history:**
- Left medial gaiter region venous leg ulcer
- Present for 11 months; previous courses of compression therapy partial response only
- Most recent breakdown 16/03/2026; current episode managed inpatient since admission 12/05/2026

**Inpatient management:**
- Doppler ABPI on admission: right 1.1, left 1.0 (suitable for compression)
- Confirmed venous aetiology on duplex scan
- 4-layer compression bandaging applied twice weekly
- Iodoform pack applied daily for first 5 days then alternate-day dressing
- Antibiotics (flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS) 7-day course completed 19/05/2026 for cellulitis
- Wound swab grew Staphylococcus aureus sensitive to flucloxacillin

**Wound assessment on discharge:**
- Wound size reduced from 6 x 4 cm to 4 x 3 cm over 14 days
- 80% granulation tissue, 20% slough
- Moderate serous exudate
- Surrounding skin: macerated; barrier cream applied
- No active infection; ankle oedema reduced

**Current dressing regimen:**
- Cleanse with saline
- Apply hydrofibre primary dressing
- Foam secondary dressing
- 4-layer compression bandaging (Profore)
- Frequency: twice weekly (Mondays and Thursdays)

**Past medical history:**
- Chronic venous insufficiency
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus, on metformin 1 g BD; last HbA1c 58
- Hypertension, on ramipril 5 mg daily
- Atrial fibrillation, on apixaban 5 mg BD

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Home circumstances:**
- Lives alone in ground-floor flat
- Daughter visits daily
- Mobile with single point stick indoors; rollator outdoors
- Reasonable cognition; manages basic ADLs independently

**Action requested from community nursing team:**
- Continue 4-layer compression dressing twice weekly using Profore system
- Monitor for signs of infection (increased pain, erythema, purulent exudate, pyrexia)
- Photograph wound weekly to monitor healing trajectory
- Reassess ABPI every 3 months
- Refer back to tissue viability service if wound deteriorates, becomes infected, or fails to heal within 6 weeks

**Writing task:** Write a transfer letter to the senior community nurse at North Riverside District Nursing Hub asking the team to take over Mr Adetola's wound care. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Henry Adetola, aged 78, is being transferred from the acute tissue viability service to community nursing care.
He has a left medial gaiter venous leg ulcer that has been present for 11 months.
He was admitted on 12 May 2026 with cellulitis around the wound.
Doppler ABPI on admission confirmed suitability for compression therapy.
Duplex scan confirmed a venous aetiology.
A 7-day course of flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily was completed for the cellulitis.
The wound has reduced in size from 6 by 4 cm to 4 by 3 cm over 14 days.
It is now 80 percent granulation tissue with 20 percent slough and moderate serous exudate.
There is no active infection and ankle oedema has reduced.
Current dressing regimen is hydrofibre primary, foam secondary, and 4-layer compression bandaging twice weekly.
Past medical history includes chronic venous insufficiency, type 2 diabetes, hypertension and atrial fibrillation.
He lives alone in a ground-floor flat with daily support from his daughter.
The community nursing team is asked to continue twice weekly Profore compression dressings.
Monitoring for signs of infection and weekly photography of the wound are requested.
Re-referral to tissue viability is requested if the wound fails to heal within 6 weeks or deteriorates.

scenarios.title: Referral to social worker: elderly falls and social risk
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Marjorie Pearson  
**DOB:** 02/02/1939 (aged 87)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Discharge planning, Ward 12.

**Reason for letter:**  
Referral to the Hospital Social Work Team for assessment of social care needs prior to anticipated discharge home. Mrs Pearson is medically optimised for discharge but has significant social risk factors that have escalated during this admission.

**Admission summary:**
- Admitted 18/05/2026 following her third unwitnessed fall at home in the past 2 months
- This fall: fractured neck of left humerus (managed conservatively in sling)
- No head injury; CT head normal
- Previous fall (14/04/2026) resulted in colles fracture, treated in plaster
- A&E attendance prior to that (08/03/2026) for a 'long lie' fall, no fracture

**Current functional status:**
- Mobility deteriorated significantly during admission
- Pre-admission: independent mobile with rollator indoors
- Currently: requires one-person assistance with all transfers; uses Zimmer frame
- Independent with feeding when assisted to position
- Requires assistance with washing, dressing (slow due to sling)
- Continent of urine and faeces with prompting

**Cognitive assessment:**
- MOCA score 22/30 (mild cognitive impairment)
- Insight into safety risks limited
- Refused offer of overnight carer at previous discharge in April

**Home circumstances:**
- Lives alone in two-bedroom semi-detached house
- Two flights of stairs to bedroom and bathroom
- No formal carer support; daughter visits weekly (lives 90 minutes away)
- Pendant alarm not worn consistently (found on bedside table during 'long lie' incident)
- House cluttered; multiple rugs and trailing flexes noted at home visit by OT on 24/05/2026

**Multidisciplinary input:**
- Physiotherapy: ongoing for falls prevention
- Occupational therapy: home visit revealed multiple environmental hazards
- Pharmacy: medication review identified zopiclone 3.75 mg nocte (5+ years), now being weaned

**Past medical history:**
- Osteoporosis (T-score -3.2 lumbar spine), on alendronic acid weekly
- Mild cognitive impairment
- Hypertension
- Chronic insomnia (on zopiclone, being weaned)

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action requested from social work team:**
- Comprehensive social care needs assessment
- Consideration of intermediate care placement vs supported discharge home
- Assessment for personal alarm and tele-care
- Family involvement in best interests discussion
- Safeguarding consideration in view of escalating falls and refusal of support

**Writing task:** Write a referral letter to Ms Aisha Kowalski, Senior Social Worker, Hospital Social Work Team, requesting assessment. Use letter prose. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mrs Marjorie Pearson, aged 87, was admitted on 18 May 2026 following her third unwitnessed fall in two months.
This admission was due to a fractured neck of left humerus, being managed conservatively in a sling.
She has had three falls in the past two months; the most recent prior fall caused a colles fracture.
Her mobility has deteriorated significantly during this admission, with assistance now required for all transfers.
She has mild cognitive impairment with a MOCA score of 22 out of 30 and limited insight into safety risks.
She refused an offer of overnight carer support at her previous discharge in April.
She lives alone in a two-bedroom house with two flights of stairs to her bedroom and bathroom.
Her daughter is her only family contact and lives 90 minutes away with weekly visits.
She has a pendant alarm but does not wear it consistently.
Occupational therapy home visit identified multiple environmental hazards including rugs and trailing flexes.
Her medication is being reviewed and zopiclone is being weaned.
Past medical history includes osteoporosis, mild cognitive impairment, hypertension and chronic insomnia.
A comprehensive social care needs assessment is requested.
Consideration of intermediate care placement versus supported discharge home is requested.
Safeguarding review is suggested in view of escalating falls and refusal of support.

scenarios.title: Routine referral: chronic migraine with medication overuse
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Catherine Wallis  
**DOB:** 04/09/1981 (aged 44)  
**Occupation:** Primary school teacher  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026

**Reason for today's visit:**  
Request for specialist neurology review of long-standing headaches now occurring on most days of the week.

**Subjective:**
- 3-year history of headaches, originally one to two per week
- Now 22 to 25 headache days per month over the past 4 months
- Pain bilateral, throbbing, frontal and bitemporal
- Associated photophobia and phonophobia on bad days; nausea occasional
- Triggers identified: poor sleep, missed meals, stress at work
- Headache diary kept over past 8 weeks
- Codeine-containing analgesia self-purchased and used 4 to 5 days per week

**Objective:**
- BP 132/82 mmHg, pulse 76 regular
- BMI 24
- Fundi clear; no papilloedema
- Cranial nerve examination unremarkable
- No focal neurological deficit
- No neck stiffness or photophobia today
- Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable

**Past medical history:**
- Migraine with aura (diagnosed 2018)
- Anxiety, on sertraline 50 mg daily since 2022
- Iron-deficiency anaemia in 2024, treated, resolved

**Current medications:**
- Sertraline 50 mg daily
- Co-codamol 30/500 mg, self-purchased, 4-5 days/week
- Sumatriptan 50 mg PRN (4-6 doses/month)
- Ferrous fumarate 210 mg daily (preventative)

**Social history:**
- Married with two children
- Non-smoker; alcohol 4 units per week

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Plan from today:**
- Refer to neurology for medication-overuse headache review and consideration of preventative therapy
- Advise gradual reduction of codeine-containing analgesia
- Continue headache diary
- Review in 6 weeks

**Writing task:** Write a routine referral letter to Dr Rachael Lin, Consultant Neurologist, City General Hospital, requesting assessment and management of likely medication-overuse headache superimposed on chronic migraine. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mrs Catherine Wallis, aged 44, primary school teacher, presents with chronic migraine now associated with medication overuse.
She has a three-year history of migraine that has escalated to 22 to 25 headache days per month over the past four months.
Her headaches are bilateral, throbbing, frontal and bitemporal with associated photophobia and phonophobia.
Triggers identified include poor sleep, missed meals and work stress.
She uses codeine-containing analgesia four to five days per week, self-purchased over the counter.
She uses sumatriptan 50 mg as required for severe attacks at four to six doses per month.
Blood pressure was 132 over 82 mmHg, pulse 76 regular, and body mass index 24.
Fundoscopy revealed no papilloedema and cranial nerve examination was unremarkable.
No focal neurological deficit or neck stiffness was identified.
Past medical history includes migraine with aura since 2018 and anxiety on sertraline 50 mg daily.
Iron-deficiency anaemia in 2024 has resolved.
Gradual reduction of codeine analgesia has been advised, and the headache diary will be continued.

scenarios.title: Urgent referral: suspected anterior uveitis with ankylosing spondylitis
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Joshua Quinn  
**DOB:** 28/02/1990 (aged 36)  
**Occupation:** Electrician  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026, 11:40 hrs

**Reason for today's visit:**  
Walk-in consultation for sudden onset painful red right eye.

**Subjective:**
- 48-hour history of painful, watering, red right eye
- Severe photophobia; cannot tolerate normal indoor lighting
- Blurred vision in the right eye
- No discharge; no contact lens use
- Mild headache same side
- No nausea, no flashing lights, no floaters
- Two similar episodes in past 4 years, both resolved with topical steroids
- Long-standing lower back stiffness and morning pain since age 22; never investigated

**Objective:**
- Right eye: marked conjunctival injection, particularly limbal (ciliary flush)
- Anterior chamber: cells visible on slit-lamp; small hypopyon at base
- Pupil sluggish on the right; visual acuity reduced to 6/12 right, 6/6 left
- Intra-ocular pressure: 16 mmHg right, 14 mmHg left
- Left eye: normal
- Forward flexion of lumbar spine: 6 cm Schober's test (limited)
- Sacroiliac joint tenderness bilaterally on direct pressure

**Past medical history:**
- Recurrent low back stiffness since teenage years
- No previous joint surgery
- No previous eye surgery

**Family history:**
- Father has ankylosing spondylitis, HLA-B27 positive

**Social history:**
- Lives with partner; works as an electrician
- Non-smoker; alcohol 6 units/week

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action taken at surgery:**
- Topical cyclopentolate 1% applied to right eye for pupillary dilatation
- Telephoned on-call ophthalmology registrar at Royal Northern Hospital
- Patient given urgent appointment same day

**Writing task:** Write an urgent referral letter to Dr Oluwa Adeleke, on-call Ophthalmology Registrar, Royal Northern Hospital Eye Casualty, requesting same-day assessment of suspected anterior uveitis and a likely first presentation of ankylosing spondylitis. Use letter prose. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Joshua Quinn, aged 36, electrician, attended walk-in surgery with a painful red right eye.
He has a 48-hour history of pain, watering and marked photophobia in the right eye.
He reports blurred vision in the right eye but denies discharge, flashing lights or floaters.
He has had two similar episodes in the past four years, both responsive to topical steroids.
He has had long-standing lower back stiffness and morning pain since the age of 22, never investigated.
His father has HLA-B27 positive ankylosing spondylitis.
Examination of the right eye revealed marked conjunctival injection with ciliary flush, cells in the anterior chamber and a small hypopyon.
Right visual acuity was reduced to 6 over 12; the left eye and intra-ocular pressures were normal.
Schober's test showed limited forward flexion of 6 cm with bilateral sacroiliac joint tenderness.
Topical cyclopentolate 1 percent has been applied at the surgery and the on-call ophthalmology team has been alerted.
He lives with his partner and is a non-smoker with a moderate alcohol intake.
There are no known drug allergies.

scenarios.title: Discharge to GP: brief admission with suspected pulmonary embolus
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Aisha Rashid  
**DOB:** 11/01/1957 (aged 69)  
**Admission date:** 23/05/2026  
**Discharge date:** 26/05/2026 (today)

**Reason for admission:**  
Acute pleuritic chest pain and breathlessness; CT pulmonary angiogram confirmed segmental pulmonary embolus.

**Presenting complaint on admission:**
- 24-hour history of right-sided pleuritic chest pain
- Acute breathlessness on minimal exertion
- No haemoptysis
- Returned from a long-haul flight 5 days previously

**Investigations on admission:**
- ECG: sinus tachycardia, rate 108; no acute ST changes
- D-dimer: 4200 ng/mL (markedly raised)
- Wells score: 4.5 (PE likely)
- CT pulmonary angiogram: segmental pulmonary embolus in the right lower lobe; no right heart strain
- Troponin: not raised
- Renal function: creatinine 84, eGFR >60
- Full blood count and clotting normal

**Inpatient management:**
- Oxygen 2 L/min for first 24 hours
- Apixaban 10 mg twice daily commenced on day 1
- IV paracetamol then oral for chest pain
- Mobilised gradually

**Progress:**
- Pain settled by day 2 with oral analgesia
- Oxygen weaned by day 2
- Saturations 96 percent on room air by day 3
- Mobile independently
- No further chest pain or breathlessness

**At discharge:**
- Apyrexial, observations stable
- Saturations 97 percent on room air
- Mobilising on the ward without limitation
- Discharge medications: apixaban 10 mg twice daily for the first seven days from start of treatment, then 5 mg twice daily for total 6 months

**Past medical history:**
- Hypertension, on amlodipine 5 mg daily
- Hypothyroidism, on levothyroxine 100 micrograms daily

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Follow-up plan:**
- Outpatient review in respiratory clinic in 8 weeks
- GP to ensure apixaban dose-step-down (from 10 mg BD to 5 mg BD) occurs on day 7 of treatment (26 May was day 4)
- Patient educated on signs of recurrence and bleeding risk

**Writing task:** Write a discharge letter to Dr Imran Khan, GP at The Meadows Surgery, requesting ongoing care including the apixaban dose-step-down. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mrs Aisha Rashid was admitted on 23 May 2026 with acute pleuritic chest pain and breathlessness following a recent long-haul flight.
Computed tomography pulmonary angiography confirmed a segmental pulmonary embolus in the right lower lobe with no right heart strain.
Her D-dimer was 4200 ng per millilitre and her Wells score was 4.5.
Apixaban 10 mg twice daily was commenced on day 1 and she received supplemental oxygen for the first 24 hours.
Her chest pain settled by day 2 and oxygen was weaned by day 3 with saturations of 97 percent on room air at discharge.
She is mobilising independently without breathlessness at the time of discharge.
Past medical history includes hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily and hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 100 micrograms daily.
She is discharged on apixaban 10 mg twice daily for the first seven days, then 5 mg twice daily for a total of six months.
The dose step-down from 10 mg twice daily to 5 mg twice daily occurs on day 7 of treatment, which is in three days' time.
Outpatient respiratory review has been booked for eight weeks.
She has been educated on signs of recurrence and bleeding risk.
There are no known drug allergies.

scenarios.title: Routine referral: paediatric eczema for dermatology specialist input

case_notes_structured.sentence: Master Oliver Hughes, aged seven years and ten months, has atopic eczema poorly controlled by primary-care treatment.
