scenarios.title: Communication to GP: salbutamol overuse identified at repeat dispensing
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Miss Hannah Reilly  
**DOB:** 14/05/2002 (aged 24)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Community pharmacy, repeat prescription dispensing.

**Reason for letter:**  
To flag a pattern of excessive short-acting beta-2 agonist use identified during repeat dispensing, and to request urgent review of asthma control.

**Dispensing history flagged:**
- Salbutamol 100 microgram inhaler: collected 12 inhalers in the past 12 months (typical NICE guidance: review if 3 or more inhalers per year)
- Last collection: today, requesting 2 inhalers simultaneously
- Beclometasone 200 microgram inhaler (preventer): last collected 9 months ago, only 1 inhaler in past 12 months
- Asthma action plan: not in pharmacy record

**Patient interview today:**
- Reports using salbutamol several times every day, sometimes 8 to 10 puffs in an hour during attacks
- Two A&E attendances in the past 6 months for acute exacerbations (one required prednisolone burst)
- Reports cough and breathlessness at night two to three nights per week
- Has stopped using beclometasone inhaler 'because it doesn't help quickly'
- Smokes 10 cigarettes per day; vaping additionally
- Triggers identified: cold air, exercise, perfume, cat dander (lives with two cats)
- No formal review with GP in past 18 months

**Action taken at pharmacy:**
- Refused supply of 2 simultaneous salbutamol inhalers; supplied one with verbal advice
- Inhaler technique check — patient using salbutamol via spacer correctly but no spacer for beclometasone
- Reinforced importance of preventer therapy; demonstrated correct beclometasone technique
- Issued asthma action plan template (BTS pediatric template adapted for young adults)
- Advised same-day urgent GP appointment
- Offered smoking cessation referral

**Current medications (all repeat):**
- Salbutamol 100 microgram inhaler PRN (over-dispensed)
- Beclometasone 200 microgram inhaler, two puffs BD (under-dispensed)
- Combined oral contraceptive pill

**Past medical history:**
- Asthma (diagnosed age 5)
- Allergic rhinitis

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action requested from GP:**
- Urgent asthma review (same week)
- Consideration of inhaled corticosteroid plus long-acting beta-2 agonist combination therapy if appropriate
- Re-engagement in structured asthma management with action plan
- Smoking cessation support

**Writing task:** Write a letter to Dr Helena Park, GP at Riverside Medical Centre, alerting her to the salbutamol-overuse pattern and requesting urgent asthma review. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Miss Hannah Reilly, aged 24, presented at the pharmacy today requesting two simultaneous salbutamol inhalers.
She has collected twelve salbutamol inhalers in the past twelve months, well above the recommended threshold for review.
Her preventer beclometasone inhaler has been markedly under-collected, with only one inhaler in the past twelve months.
She reports using salbutamol several times daily, with up to ten puffs in an hour during attacks.
She has had two A&E attendances in the past six months for acute asthma exacerbations.
She has stopped using her beclometasone inhaler because she perceives it does not help quickly.
She has nocturnal cough and breathlessness two to three nights per week.
She smokes ten cigarettes per day and additionally vapes, with triggers including cold air, exercise, perfume and cat dander.
She has not had a formal asthma review with her GP for eighteen months.
Two simultaneous salbutamol inhalers were refused today; one inhaler was supplied with verbal advice and inhaler technique was checked.
She has been advised to attend her GP same-day and offered smoking cessation referral.
She has no known drug allergies.

scenarios.title: Communication to oncology pharmacy: high-dose methotrexate dosing query
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Lukasz Nowak  
**DOB:** 02/03/1971 (aged 55)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Hospital ward 14, hospital pharmacy clinical-check intervention.

**Reason for letter:**  
To alert the oncology pharmacist team to deteriorating renal function in Mr Nowak prior to his next scheduled cycle of high-dose intravenous methotrexate, and to request review of dosing and folinic-acid rescue plan.

**Disease background:**
- Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with central nervous system involvement
- Currently on cycle 4 of CNS prophylaxis with high-dose methotrexate (3 g/m^2 every 14 days)
- Cycle 5 scheduled for 28/05/2026

**Concern identified at clinical check:**
- Pre-cycle renal function trajectory:
  - Cycle 3 (14/05): creatinine 92, eGFR 78
  - Cycle 4 (21/05): creatinine 108, eGFR 65
  - Today (26/05, pre-cycle 5): creatinine 138, eGFR 49
- Methotrexate excretion is renally dependent; eGFR <60 requires dose reduction or interval extension per local protocol
- Cycle 4 methotrexate plasma level at 48 hours was 0.32 micromol/L (above target <0.2)
- Folinic acid rescue extended in cycle 4 from 72 to 96 hours

**Other relevant findings:**
- Mr Nowak is tolerating treatment with nausea controlled by ondansetron
- No mucositis to date
- Has been advised on adequate hydration (3 L/day) and urinary alkalinisation
- Current urinary pH 7.6 (target >7)

**Current medications:**
- High-dose methotrexate (cycle 5 scheduled 28/05)
- Folinic acid rescue per protocol
- Sodium bicarbonate for urinary alkalinisation
- Ondansetron 8 mg PRN nausea
- Paracetamol 1 g QDS PRN
- Allopurinol 300 mg daily (tumour lysis prophylaxis)

**Past medical history:**
- Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (diagnosed January 2026)
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus, on metformin 1 g BD
- Hypertension, on lisinopril 20 mg daily (held during chemotherapy)

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action requested from oncology pharmacy:**
- Review of cycle 5 methotrexate dosing in view of declining renal function
- Consideration of interval extension or dose reduction per local high-dose methotrexate protocol
- Pre-cycle hydration and alkalinisation plan re-confirmed
- Extended folinic acid rescue regimen if cycle 5 proceeds
- Continued daily renal function monitoring during the cycle

**Writing task:** Write a letter to Dr Priya Singh, Lead Oncology Pharmacist, City General Hospital Oncology Day Unit, alerting her to Mr Nowak's deteriorating renal function before cycle 5 high-dose methotrexate. Use letter prose. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Lukasz Nowak, aged 55, is on cycle 4 of CNS prophylaxis with high-dose intravenous methotrexate for diffuse large B-cell lymphoma.
Cycle 5 is scheduled for 28 May 2026 and clinical check today has identified deteriorating renal function.
Creatinine has risen from 92 at cycle 3 to 108 at cycle 4 and 138 today, with eGFR now 49.
Cycle 4 methotrexate plasma level at 48 hours was 0.32 micromol/L, above the target of less than 0.2.
Folinic acid rescue was extended from 72 to 96 hours during cycle 4 because of delayed clearance.
He is tolerating treatment with controlled nausea on ondansetron and no mucositis to date.
He is adequately hydrated at 3 L per day with urinary alkalinisation in place; today's urinary pH is 7.6.
Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes on metformin 1 g twice daily and hypertension on lisinopril 20 mg daily, currently held.
Review of cycle 5 dosing in the context of declining renal function is requested.
Consideration of interval extension or dose reduction per local protocol is suggested.
An extended folinic acid rescue regimen and continued daily renal function monitoring are requested if cycle 5 proceeds.
There are no known drug allergies.

scenarios.title: Communication to GP: SSRI-induced hyponatraemia in elderly patient
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Eileen Murphy  
**DOB:** 22/11/1942 (aged 83)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Community pharmacy medicines-use review.

**Reason for letter:**  
To highlight symptomatic features and recent laboratory data suggesting SSRI-induced hyponatraemia following recent dose increase of citalopram, and to request review of antidepressant strategy.

**Pharmacy interaction today:**
- Mrs Murphy attended for monthly prescription collection and asked about new symptoms
- New symptoms: fatigue, mild confusion, two unwitnessed falls in the past 10 days (no fracture)
- Daughter (who accompanied her) reports increased drowsiness
- Pharmacy noted that citalopram was titrated from 20 mg to 40 mg on 12/05/2026 by GP following depressive symptoms
- Sodium check on 22/05/2026 (community phlebotomy, results visible on Summary Care Record): 128 mmol/L (baseline 138 on 28/04/2026)

**Mrs Murphy's other medications (potential contributors):**
- Bendroflumethiazide 2.5 mg daily (long-standing, for hypertension)
- Citalopram 40 mg daily (recently uptitrated)
- Lansoprazole 30 mg daily
- Aspirin 75 mg daily
- Adcal-D3 two tablets daily

**Symptoms timeline:**
- Pre-titration (early May): no fatigue, no falls
- Days 7-10 post-titration: fatigue noted
- Days 10-14 post-titration: mild confusion and first fall
- Today (day 14): persistent symptoms with second fall

**Past medical history:**
- Depression (treated since 2024)
- Hypertension
- Osteoporosis
- Mild cognitive impairment

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action taken at pharmacy today:**
- Withheld dispensing of citalopram 40 mg pending GP review (patient consented)
- Advised same-day GP appointment
- Reinforced importance of monitoring fluid intake and seeking medical advice if symptoms worsen
- Provided written explanation of SIADH/hyponatraemia for patient and daughter

**Action requested from GP:**
- Same-day review of recent symptoms with consideration of repeat U&E
- Review of citalopram dose; consider reverting to 20 mg or switch to alternative agent
- Review of bendroflumethiazide (additional hyponatraemia risk)
- Fluid restriction advice if confirmed SIADH

**Writing task:** Write a letter to Dr Akinwale Adeyemi, GP at Riverside Medical Centre, alerting him to suspected SSRI-induced hyponatraemia following the recent citalopram dose increase. Use letter prose. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mrs Eileen Murphy, aged 83, attended the pharmacy today with new symptoms following a recent increase in citalopram dose.
Citalopram was titrated from 20 mg to 40 mg daily on 12 May 2026.
New symptoms include fatigue, mild confusion and two unwitnessed falls in the past 10 days with no fracture.
Her daughter reports increased drowsiness since the dose was changed.
Sodium was 128 mmol/L on 22 May 2026, having been 138 mmol/L on 28 April 2026.
She also takes bendroflumethiazide 2.5 mg daily, which is an additional risk factor for hyponatraemia.
Symptoms have followed a clear post-titration timeline, escalating from fatigue at day 7 to falls and confusion by day 14.
Past medical history includes depression treated since 2024, hypertension, osteoporosis and mild cognitive impairment.
Today's citalopram 40 mg supply has been withheld pending GP review with the patient's consent.
She has been advised to attend her GP the same day and offered written explanation of suspected hyponatraemia.
Review of citalopram dose and bendroflumethiazide is requested.
Repeat sodium check and fluid restriction guidance are recommended if SIADH is confirmed.

scenarios.title: Response to GP: post-myocardial-infarction medication optimisation
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Donald McKenzie  
**DOB:** 14/06/1955 (aged 70)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Cardiology pharmacy clinic, hospital outpatients.

**Reason for letter:**  
Response to Dr Imran Khan, GP at The Meadows Surgery, regarding his letter of 19/05/2026 requesting optimisation of post-myocardial-infarction medications and review of statin intolerance.

**Background from GP letter (received 19/05/2026):**
- ST-elevation myocardial infarction 02/04/2026, treated with primary percutaneous coronary intervention to left anterior descending artery
- Discharged on aspirin 75 mg daily, ticagrelor 90 mg twice daily, bisoprolol 5 mg daily, ramipril 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 80 mg daily
- Reports muscle aches on atorvastatin 80 mg; creatine kinase mildly raised at 412 IU/L (upper limit 250)
- GP requested cardiology pharmacy clinic review for statin optimisation and consideration of ezetimibe

**Today's clinic findings:**
- Mr McKenzie reports significant muscle aches in both thighs since starting atorvastatin 80 mg
- No dark urine; no severe pain
- Compliance with all other medications confirmed
- Blood pressure 122/74; pulse 64
- LDL cholesterol on current therapy: 2.4 mmol/L (target <1.8 post-MI per NICE)
- HbA1c 42 mmol/mol (non-diabetic range)
- Renal function: eGFR 72
- Liver function tests normal

**Action taken in clinic:**
- Reduced atorvastatin to 40 mg daily (dose-related myalgia common at 80 mg)
- Added ezetimibe 10 mg daily for LDL target attainment
- Continued aspirin, ticagrelor (until 12 months post-PCI), bisoprolol, ramipril
- Smoking cessation reinforced
- 6-week follow-up arranged in pharmacy clinic

**Plan agreed:**
- Recheck LDL and creatine kinase in 6 weeks
- If LDL still above 1.8 mmol/L, consider PCSK9 inhibitor referral per NICE TA694
- If creatine kinase rises further or muscle symptoms worsen, hold statin and review

**Past medical history:**
- ST-elevation myocardial infarction (April 2026)
- Hypertension since 2018
- Mild chronic kidney disease (CKD stage 2)

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action requested from GP:**
- Repeat LDL cholesterol and creatine kinase in 6 weeks
- Continue current regimen as documented
- Re-refer if muscle symptoms recur or worsen

**Writing task:** Write a reply letter to Dr Imran Khan summarising today's pharmacist-led clinic and the agreed plan for statin optimisation. Use letter prose. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Donald McKenzie, aged 70, attended the cardiology pharmacy clinic today in response to Dr Khan's letter of 19 May 2026.
He had an ST-elevation myocardial infarction on 2 April 2026, treated with primary percutaneous coronary intervention to the left anterior descending artery.
He reports significant bilateral thigh aches since starting atorvastatin 80 mg, with a mildly raised creatine kinase of 412 IU/L.
His current LDL cholesterol is 2.4 mmol/L, above the post-MI target of less than 1.8.
Blood pressure today was 122 over 74 mmHg with a pulse of 64 beats per minute.
Atorvastatin has been reduced to 40 mg daily today, and ezetimibe 10 mg daily has been added.
Aspirin, ticagrelor, bisoprolol and ramipril are continued without change.
A 6-week follow-up appointment in the pharmacy clinic has been arranged.
Repeat LDL cholesterol and creatine kinase in 6 weeks are requested from the GP.
If LDL remains above target, PCSK9 inhibitor referral per NICE TA694 will be considered.
Past medical history includes hypertension since 2018 and CKD stage 2.
There are no known drug allergies.

scenarios.title: Discharge to GP: brief admission with stable urinary tract infection
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Beatrice Olson  
**DOB:** 03/06/1944 (aged 81)  
**Admission date:** 24/05/2026  
**Discharge date:** 26/05/2026 (today)

**Reason for admission:**  
Acute confusion in the context of urinary tract infection.

**Presenting complaint on admission:**
- 24-hour history of acute confusion noted by daughter at home visit
- Urinary frequency and dysuria for 3 days
- Mild fever; no rigors
- Reduced oral intake

**Investigations on admission:**
- Urine dipstick: leukocytes ++, nitrites positive, blood +
- Mid-stream urine: growing Escherichia coli sensitive to nitrofurantoin
- White cell count 14.8; CRP 96
- Renal function: creatinine 89, eGFR 55 (baseline 60)
- Sodium and potassium within range
- Chest X-ray clear

**Inpatient management:**
- IV ceftriaxone 1 g once daily for 24 hours, then oral nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily
- IV fluid replacement on day 1
- Paracetamol 1 g QDS for fever
- Confusion-screen bloods normal (B12, folate, TSH)
- Memory clinic referral not indicated; confusion resolved with infection treatment

**Progress:**
- Cognitive function returned to baseline by day 2
- Apyrexial from day 2
- Tolerating oral intake and antibiotics
- Mobile with frame at baseline level
- Independently transferring; continent of urine

**At discharge:**
- Cognitive and functional status returned to baseline
- Observations stable
- Oral nitrofurantoin to complete a 5-day course (3 days remaining)
- Returning home with daughter to provide supervision

**Past medical history:**
- Mild cognitive impairment (donepezil since 2024)
- Atrial fibrillation, on apixaban 2.5 mg BD
- Hypertension, on amlodipine 5 mg daily

**Allergies:** Penicillin (rash, age 18).

**Discharge medications:**
- Nitrofurantoin 100 mg BD for 3 more days
- Donepezil 10 mg nocte (continued)
- Apixaban 2.5 mg BD (continued)
- Amlodipine 5 mg daily (continued)
- Paracetamol 1 g QDS PRN

**Home circumstances:**
- Daughter staying with her for the next 5 days
- District nurse not normally involved; daughter manages care
- Pendant alarm in place

**Required follow-up:**
- GP review at 2 weeks to assess full cognitive recovery
- Routine repeat urinalysis if symptoms recur
- No district nurse input required at this stage

**Writing task:** Write a discharge letter to Dr Sarah Ahmed, Mrs Olson's GP at Riverside Medical Centre, summarising the brief admission and ongoing care arrangements. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mrs Beatrice Olson was admitted on 24 May 2026 with acute confusion in the context of a urinary tract infection.
Mid-stream urine grew Escherichia coli sensitive to nitrofurantoin.
She received intravenous ceftriaxone for 24 hours, followed by oral nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily.
She received intravenous fluids on day 1 and paracetamol for fever.
Her cognitive function returned to baseline by day 2 and she became apyrexial on the same day.
She is mobile with her frame at her baseline level and continent of urine.
She is discharged on oral nitrofurantoin to complete a 5-day course with three days remaining.
Past medical history includes mild cognitive impairment on donepezil, atrial fibrillation on apixaban and hypertension on amlodipine.
She has a documented penicillin allergy, which caused a rash at age 18.
Her daughter will stay with her for the next five days to provide supervision.
GP review at two weeks is requested to assess full cognitive recovery.
No district nurse input is required at this stage.

scenarios.title: Transfer to inpatient mental health unit: deteriorating psychosis
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Daniel Hartley  
**DOB:** 19/08/1996 (aged 29)  
**Transfer date:** 26/05/2026 (today)  
**Setting:** Crisis resolution and home treatment team, transfer to acute mental health inpatient unit.

**Reason for transfer:**  
Deterioration in mental state despite intensive community support; admission to acute inpatient unit at Holly House Hospital for further assessment, stabilisation and consideration of medication review.

**Episode background:**
- First episode psychosis diagnosis April 2024; initial treatment with risperidone
- Switched to aripiprazole 15 mg daily in 2025 due to side effects (sexual dysfunction)
- Stable for 8 months on aripiprazole until February 2026
- Recent stressors: relationship breakdown, loss of part-time job, escalating cannabis use
- Referred to crisis team on 12/05/2026 by GP

**Crisis-team interventions over past 14 days:**
- Twice-daily visits for first week, then daily
- Aripiprazole dose increased to 20 mg daily from 14/05/2026
- Lorazepam 1 mg PRN nocte for sleep and anxiety
- Brief intervention regarding cannabis use
- Mother actively involved with team consent

**Current mental state today:**
- Increasing agitation and pacing
- Auditory hallucinations: voices commenting on his actions and accusing him of being a 'fraud'
- Persecutory ideation: believes neighbours are filming him through the wall
- Sleep: 2-3 hours per night for past 5 nights
- Mood: low but no expressed suicidal ideation
- No active plans to harm self or others; no recent self-harm
- Insight markedly diminished

**Risk assessment:**
- Risk to self: moderate (sleep deprivation, low mood, disorganised behaviour)
- Risk to others: low currently, but increasing agitation
- Risk of disengagement: high (refused two visits this week)
- Risk of substance use escalation: high

**Social history:**
- Lives alone in a one-bedroom flat
- Mother (next of kin) lives 30 minutes away and supportive
- Smokes cannabis daily, recently increased to 3-4 joints per day
- Smokes 10 cigarettes per day
- Has been declining offered medication for last 3 days

**Current medications:**
- Aripiprazole 20 mg daily (compliance uncertain)
- Lorazepam 1 mg PRN nocte

**Past psychiatric history:**
- First episode psychosis April 2024 (no prior episodes)
- No prior admissions to psychiatric units

**Past medical history:**
- Unremarkable

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action taken today:**
- Voluntary admission agreed after mother and crisis team discussion
- Mental Health Act assessment not required at this stage
- Mother to accompany to ward
- Aripiprazole this morning's dose taken under supervision

**Writing task:** Write a transfer letter to Senior Charge Nurse Mr David Olaniyi at Holly House Hospital acute inpatient unit (Ward 4), summarising the current presentation, recent crisis team interventions and outstanding care needs. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Daniel Hartley, aged 29, is being voluntarily admitted from the crisis team to the acute inpatient unit for stabilisation.
He has had first-episode psychosis since April 2024, currently on aripiprazole 20 mg daily.
His mental state has deteriorated over the past two weeks despite intensive crisis team input.
Recent stressors include relationship breakdown, job loss and escalating cannabis use to three to four joints per day.
Current presentation includes auditory hallucinations and persecutory ideation about neighbours filming him.
He is sleeping only two to three hours per night with marked agitation and pacing.
His insight is markedly diminished and he has refused two crisis team visits in the past week.
Risk to self is moderate; risk to others is low at present but increasing.
He has no expressed suicidal ideation and no active plans to harm self or others.
His mother is his next of kin and has been actively involved with the team and consents to admission.
Compliance with aripiprazole has been uncertain over the past three days; this morning's dose was taken under supervision.
He has no prior psychiatric admissions and his physical health is unremarkable.

scenarios.title: Communication to school nurse: paediatric anaphylaxis preparedness
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Master Ethan Bryant  
**DOB:** 02/03/2017 (aged 9)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Children's allergy clinic, paediatric outpatients.

**Reason for letter:**  
To inform the school nursing team at Holly Lane Primary School of Ethan's confirmed peanut anaphylaxis and the updated allergy action plan for the school setting.

**Allergy background:**
- Confirmed peanut allergy on skin-prick testing (peanut wheal 10 mm, control negative)
- Specific IgE to peanut: 65 kU/L (very high risk)
- Component-resolved testing: Ara h 2 positive (high risk for systemic reaction)

**Anaphylaxis history:**
- One previous anaphylactic episode in November 2025
  - Trace peanut exposure at a friend's birthday party
  - Symptoms: facial swelling, urticaria, wheeze and vomiting
  - Treated with adrenaline auto-injector by mother, followed by A&E observation
  - No intensive care admission required
- No further episodes since

**Today's review findings:**
- Mother and Ethan attended clinic; Ethan articulate and engaged with discussion
- Both demonstrated correct use of adrenaline auto-injector technique on trainer
- Family carries two adrenaline auto-injectors at all times
- School currently does not hold a spare adrenaline auto-injector for Ethan
- New AAP (Allergy Action Plan) issued, updated for school context
- BSACI (British Society for Allergy and Clinical Immunology) age-appropriate education materials provided

**Other relevant findings:**
- Mild atopic eczema, controlled
- Mild allergic rhinitis (seasonal)
- Currently growing normally; weight on 50th centile
- No other known food allergies confirmed; sensitised to tree nuts on testing but no reaction history

**Current management plan:**
- Strict peanut avoidance
- Two adrenaline auto-injectors at home and in school bag
- Chlorphenamine syrup for mild reactions only
- Spacer and salbutamol available at school for asthma symptoms
- Annual allergy clinic review

**Allergies:**
- Peanut (anaphylaxis, confirmed)
- Sensitised to tree nuts (avoidance recommended but no reaction history)

**Action requested from school nurse:**
- Distribution of updated allergy action plan to all relevant staff (class teacher, lunchtime supervisors, after-school club leaders)
- Confirmation of two adrenaline auto-injectors in school bag and one spare held in school first-aid cupboard
- Annual staff training in adrenaline auto-injector technique (offered through allergy clinic outreach service)
- Communication with parents of classmates regarding peanut-free environment in classroom and at shared lunch areas
- Re-contact for ad-hoc training if any staff change

**Writing task:** Write a communication letter to School Nurse Mrs Caroline Westwood at Holly Lane Primary School about Ethan's allergy plan and the supports requested from the school. Use letter prose. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Master Ethan Bryant, aged 9, has confirmed peanut anaphylaxis and attended the children's allergy clinic today for review.
Diagnosis was confirmed by skin-prick testing and specific IgE of 65 kU/L, with Ara h 2 component positivity.
He had one anaphylactic episode in November 2025 after trace peanut exposure, treated with adrenaline auto-injector and A&E observation.
His mother and he demonstrated correct adrenaline auto-injector technique today on a trainer device.
The family carries two adrenaline auto-injectors at all times.
An updated allergy action plan tailored to the school setting was issued at today's review.
He has mild atopic eczema and seasonal allergic rhinitis, both well controlled.
Distribution of the updated action plan to relevant school staff is requested.
Confirmation of two auto-injectors in his bag and one spare in the school first-aid cupboard is requested.
Annual staff training in auto-injector technique is offered through the allergy clinic outreach service.
Communication with classmates' parents regarding peanut-free environments is suggested.
Ad-hoc retraining can be arranged for any new staff joining the school.

scenarios.title: Routine referral to continence service: post-stroke urinary incontinence
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Henry Wilson  
**DOB:** 16/07/1948 (aged 77)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** District nursing service, community.

**Reason for referral:**  
Urinary incontinence persisting 8 weeks post-ischaemic-stroke, with impact on rehabilitation participation, carer burden and skin integrity.

**Stroke background:**
- Right middle cerebral artery ischaemic stroke 02/04/2026
- Initial treatment with intravenous thrombolysis
- Admitted to stroke unit, discharged home 24/04/2026 after 3 weeks of inpatient rehabilitation
- Residual deficits: left arm weakness (Medical Research Council grade 3), mild dysarthria, mild cognitive slowing

**Urinary symptoms (since discharge):**
- Daily episodes of urinary incontinence, both daytime (3-4 episodes) and nocturnal (1-2 episodes)
- Mixed pattern: some urge incontinence, some functional incontinence (cannot reach toilet quickly enough due to left-sided weakness)
- No haematuria, no dysuria, no fever
- Bladder diary kept by wife over past 2 weeks
- Has been declining offered continence pads (perceived as 'degrading')

**Bladder assessment by district nurse today:**
- Urinalysis: no leukocytes, no nitrites, no blood
- Post-void residual scan: 60 mL (acceptable)
- Bladder diary shows void frequency every 1-2 hours during day; bladder capacity reduced (200-250 mL voids)
- No evidence of constipation contributing

**Functional and environmental issues:**
- Toilet upstairs; commode in living room used during day
- Wife is primary carer; reports increasing fatigue from frequent toileting assistance
- Two pressure-area redness episodes on sacrum in past month (related to incontinence)
- Skin integrity managed with barrier cream and frequent changing of underwear
- Wife declining offer of home modifications (downstairs toilet conversion) for cost reasons

**Current medications:**
- Clopidogrel 75 mg daily (post-stroke)
- Atorvastatin 80 mg daily
- Amlodipine 10 mg daily
- Ramipril 5 mg daily

**Past medical history:**
- Ischaemic stroke (April 2026)
- Hypertension since 2015
- Hypercholesterolaemia
- Mild cognitive slowing since stroke

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action taken today:**
- Bladder assessment completed and documented
- Bladder diary reviewed with wife
- Barrier cream supplied; pressure-area skin care advised
- Offered continence pads accepted by patient after discussion
- Liaison with stroke rehabilitation team about continued physiotherapy for left-sided weakness

**Action requested from continence service:**
- Specialist continence assessment within 4-6 weeks
- Consideration of bladder retraining programme appropriate for post-stroke cognition
- Assessment for pelvic floor muscle training adapted for stroke-related limitations
- Pharmacological options consideration (antimuscarinics carry cognitive risk in post-stroke patients)
- Home environmental assessment if appropriate for downstairs toilet recommendation
- Carer support information for his wife

**Writing task:** Write a routine referral letter to Specialist Continence Nurse Ms Janet Chowdhury at City Community Continence Service, requesting specialist assessment for Mr Wilson's post-stroke urinary incontinence. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Henry Wilson, aged 77, has had urinary incontinence persisting eight weeks following a right middle cerebral artery ischaemic stroke.
Residual deficits include left arm weakness, mild dysarthria and mild cognitive slowing.
He has three to four daytime and one to two nocturnal incontinence episodes daily.
The pattern is mixed urge and functional incontinence, with reduced ability to reach the toilet quickly.
Urinalysis today was negative and post-void residual scan was 60 mL.
Bladder diary shows reduced functional capacity with voids of 200 to 250 mL every one to two hours.
He has had two episodes of sacral pressure-area redness in the past month related to incontinence.
His wife is the primary carer and reports increasing fatigue from frequent toileting assistance.
He had initially declined continence pads but accepted them today after discussion.
Current medications include clopidogrel, atorvastatin, amlodipine and ramipril.
Antimuscarinic medications carry cognitive risk in post-stroke patients and require specialist consideration.
Carer support information for his wife is also requested from the continence service.

scenarios.title: Routine referral: suspected atrial fibrillation in primary care

- Chest clear; heart sounds normal; no peripheral oedema today

**Validated tools:**
- CHA2DS2-VASc score: 3 (age, female, hypertension)
- HAS-BLED score: 1

**Past medical history:**
- Hypertension (since 2018), on amlodipine 5 mg daily
- Hypercholesterolaemia, on atorvastatin 20 mg nocte
- Osteoarthritis, on paracetamol PRN

**Social history:**

- Lives with husband

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Plan from today:**

**Writing task:** Write a routine referral letter to Dr Aaron Lee, Consultant Cardiologist, City General Hospital, requesting assessment and management. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mrs Imelda Foster, aged 70, retired librarian, presented for medication review and was found to have an irregular pulse.
Electrocardiogram today confirmed atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 92 beats per minute.
She reports occasional brief, self-terminating palpitations over the past two to three months.

Blood pressure today was 138 over 82 mmHg and oxygen saturations were 97 percent on room air.

scenarios.title: Urgent referral: sudden severe headache with neurological signs

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Andrew Brennan, aged 53, software developer, attended walk-in surgery with a 2-hour history of sudden severe occipital headache.
The headache reached maximum intensity within seconds while seated at his desk and is described as the worst headache of his life.
Associated features include nausea, one episode of vomiting and photophobia, with mild developing neck stiffness.

Past medical history includes hypertension on lisinopril 20 mg daily.

An ambulance has been requested for immediate transfer to A&E for emergency CT head.
He is a smoker of 10 cigarettes per day for 30 years and drinks 10 to 12 units of alcohol per week.

scenarios.title: Response to GP: post-cardiology clinic medication update
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Lawrence Tanner  
**DOB:** 30/04/1949 (aged 76)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Cardiology outpatient clinic.

**Reason for letter:**  
Response to Dr Imran Khan at The Meadows Surgery (letter of 12/05/2026) requesting cardiology review of Mr Tanner's recent breathlessness and consideration of his heart failure medication.

**Background from GP letter (received 12/05/2026):**
- Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (LVEF 32% on most recent echocardiogram, January 2026)
- On bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily, ramipril 5 mg daily, furosemide 40 mg daily
- Recent admission (March 2026) for decompensation, successfully diuresed
- GP reports increased breathlessness over the past 4 weeks and 2 kg weight gain
- GP requesting clinic review

**Today's clinic findings:**
- Mr Tanner attended with his wife
- Reports increased dyspnoea on exertion (NYHA II to III)
- Mild ankle swelling, worse at end of day
- Sleeps on two pillows; no paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea
- BP 118/72; pulse 68 regular; SpO2 95% on room air
- BMI 28; weight 78 kg (up from 76 kg one month ago)
- JVP elevated at 4 cm above sternal angle
- Bilateral basal crackles on auscultation
- Mild pitting peripheral oedema
- Recent bloods (today): NT-proBNP 1850 ng/L (was 1100 in January), creatinine 132, eGFR 48, sodium 137, potassium 4.6
- ECG: sinus rhythm, rate 68, no acute changes
- Echocardiogram requested for repeat measurement of LVEF

**Action taken in clinic:**
- Furosemide increased to 80 mg daily for 7 days, then reassess
- Eplerenone 25 mg daily initiated
- Continue bisoprolol and ramipril
- Discussed sacubitril/valsartan as future option pending titration tolerance
- Daily weight monitoring discussed with wife
- Follow-up appointment in 2 weeks

**Past medical history:**
- Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (since 2021)
- Hypertension since 2015
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diet-controlled
- Mild chronic kidney disease (CKD stage 3a)

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action requested from GP:**
- Monitor daily weights as discussed
- Repeat U&E in 2 weeks following diuretic increase and eplerenone start (monitor potassium)
- Follow-up cardiology appointment booked for 9 June 2026
- Re-contact clinic if rapid weight gain (>2 kg in 3 days) or worsening symptoms

**Writing task:** Write a reply letter to Dr Imran Khan summarising today's clinic and the medication changes. Use letter prose. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Lawrence Tanner, aged 76, attended cardiology clinic today in response to Dr Khan's letter of 12 May 2026.
He has heart failure with reduced ejection fraction of 32 percent on his most recent echocardiogram in January 2026.
Over the past four weeks he reports increased dyspnoea on exertion progressing from NYHA II to III, with mild ankle swelling and a 2 kg weight gain.
Examination revealed bilateral basal crackles, mild pitting peripheral oedema and a jugular venous pressure elevated 4 cm above the sternal angle.
NT-proBNP has risen from 1100 to 1850 ng/L; creatinine is 132 and eGFR is 48.
Furosemide has been increased to 80 mg daily for seven days with reassessment.
Eplerenone 25 mg daily has been initiated.
Bisoprolol and ramipril are continued unchanged; sacubitril/valsartan has been discussed for future consideration.
A repeat echocardiogram has been requested and follow-up in cardiology clinic is booked for 9 June 2026.
Daily weight monitoring has been discussed with his wife.
Past medical history includes hypertension since 2015, diet-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus and mild chronic kidney disease.
Repeat urea and electrolytes in two weeks are requested from the GP to monitor potassium following diuretic increase and eplerenone start.

scenarios.title: Discharge to GP: post-surgical hernia repair
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Conor O'Riordan  
**DOB:** 22/11/1968 (aged 57)  
**Admission date:** 24/05/2026  
**Discharge date:** 26/05/2026 (today)

**Reason for admission:**  
Elective laparoscopic right inguinal hernia repair with mesh.

**Operative details:**
- Performed 24/05/2026 by Mr T Lawrence (consultant general surgeon)
- General anaesthesia, no intra-operative complications
- TAPP (transabdominal pre-peritoneal) technique with polypropylene mesh
- Three port sites closed with absorbable sutures and Steristrips

**Inpatient progress:**
- Day 0: tolerated diet, mobilised independently
- Day 1: home discharge planned but delayed by post-operative nausea (now resolved)
- Pain controlled with paracetamol 1 g QDS and ibuprofen 400 mg TDS
- Port sites clean, dry, no haematoma
- Bowels opened day 1
- Voiding without difficulty

**At discharge:**
- Afebrile, observations stable
- Mobilising independently without analgesia within last 12 hours
- Wounds clean and dry; Steristrips intact
- Patient and wife confident with self-care

**Past medical history:**
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus, on metformin 500 mg BD
- Hypertension, on amlodipine 5 mg daily

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Discharge medications:**
- Paracetamol 1 g QDS PRN
- Ibuprofen 400 mg TDS PRN with food (max 5 days)
- Lansoprazole 15 mg daily for 7 days (NSAID cover)
- Metformin 500 mg BD (continued)
- Amlodipine 5 mg daily (continued)

**Patient advice given:**
- Keep wounds dry for 5 days; can shower thereafter
- Avoid heavy lifting (>5 kg) for 2 weeks; no driving for 1 week
- Steristrips to fall off naturally within 7-10 days

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Conor O'Riordan was admitted on 24 May 2026 for an elective laparoscopic right inguinal hernia repair with mesh.
Surgery was performed under general anaesthesia using TAPP technique by Mr T Lawrence, with no intra-operative complications.
His post-operative course was uncomplicated apart from transient nausea, now resolved.
Port sites are clean, dry, with intact Steristrips and no haematoma.
He is mobilising independently and is afebrile at discharge.
He is being discharged on paracetamol PRN, ibuprofen PRN with NSAID gastric cover (lansoprazole 15 mg daily for seven days), and continues his usual metformin and amlodipine.
He has been advised to keep wounds dry for five days, avoid heavy lifting for two weeks, avoid driving for one week, and re-attend if any wound concerns.
Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus on metformin and hypertension on amlodipine.
There are no known drug allergies.
GP review at two weeks is requested for wound check and to confirm return to work.
No surgical outpatient follow-up is required unless complications arise.

scenarios.title: Pharmacy intervention: lithium toxicity risk
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Sophia Marinescu  
**DOB:** 14/01/1972 (aged 54)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Community pharmacy clinical-check intervention.

**Reason for letter:**  
To alert prescriber to a high-risk drug interaction between recently initiated bendroflumethiazide and her established lithium therapy, with symptoms suggestive of early lithium toxicity, and to request urgent review.

**Prescription history flagged:**
- Lithium carbonate 800 mg nocte (long-standing, since 2015) — last lithium level 17/04/2026 was 0.7 mmol/L (target 0.4-1.0)
- NEW: Bendroflumethiazide 2.5 mg daily, initiated by GP 13/05/2026 for blood pressure control
- Pharmacy clinical-check identified lithium-thiazide interaction with risk of increased lithium levels and toxicity

**Patient interview today:**
- Reports mild fine hand tremor for past week (new)
- Slight nausea, no vomiting
- Mild fatigue, drowsiness
- No diarrhoea, no confusion, no muscle weakness
- Continues all medications as prescribed
- No recent diet changes; no recent vomiting/diarrhoea or dehydration
- Last lithium level was within target one month ago

**Action taken at pharmacy today:**
- Bendroflumethiazide supply withheld pending GP review (patient consents)
- Lithium continued at usual dose
- Advised to attend GP same day
- Booked priority lithium level for tomorrow at the practice
- Provided written advice on lithium toxicity symptoms
- Reinforced importance of consistent fluid and salt intake

**Current medications (all repeat):**
- Lithium carbonate 800 mg nocte
- Bendroflumethiazide 2.5 mg daily (NEW, withheld today)
- Sertraline 100 mg daily
- Levothyroxine 75 micrograms daily (long-standing, for lithium-induced hypothyroidism)

**Past medical history:**
- Bipolar affective disorder (on lithium since 2015)
- Hypertension (newly diagnosed)
- Lithium-induced hypothyroidism

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action requested from GP:**
- Urgent same-day review of symptoms
- Reconsideration of bendroflumethiazide; alternative antihypertensive without lithium interaction (e.g., amlodipine)
- Confirmation of lithium level booking
- Patient education reinforcement on dehydration risk and salt intake stability

**Writing task:** Write a letter to Dr Helena Park, Mrs Marinescu's GP at Riverside Medical Centre, alerting her to the lithium-thiazide interaction and request review. Use letter prose. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mrs Sophia Marinescu, aged 54, attended the pharmacy today for repeat prescription collection.
Clinical check identified a high-risk interaction between newly initiated bendroflumethiazide and her established lithium therapy.
She has been on lithium carbonate 800 mg nightly since 2015, with the most recent level of 0.7 mmol/L within target on 17 April 2026.
Bendroflumethiazide 2.5 mg daily was initiated on 13 May 2026 by the GP for blood pressure control.
She reports new fine hand tremor for the past week, mild nausea, fatigue and drowsiness.
She denies diarrhoea, confusion or muscle weakness.
The bendroflumethiazide supply has been withheld today with her consent, pending GP review.
She has been advised to attend her GP the same day.
A priority lithium level has been booked for tomorrow at the practice.
She has been given written advice on lithium toxicity symptoms.
Other current medications include sertraline 100 mg daily and levothyroxine 75 micrograms daily for lithium-induced hypothyroidism.
Reconsideration of bendroflumethiazide and selection of an antihypertensive without lithium interaction is requested.

scenarios.title: Pharmacy communication: emergency contraception query and follow-up

case_notes_structured.sentence: Miss Iona Khoury, aged 22, attended the pharmacy today for emergency hormonal contraception.

Long-acting reversible contraception options have been discussed for future planning.
Past medical history includes occasional migraine without aura and no history of venous thromboembolism.
There are no known drug allergies.
The GP is asked to discuss long-acting reversible contraception at her next routine appointment.
Discussion of contraceptive adherence support and STI screening at routine review is suggested.

scenarios.title: Pharmacy review: paediatric inhaler technique and asthma plan
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Master Aiden Sullivan (parent: Mr Patrick Sullivan)  
**DOB:** 09/02/2015 (aged 11)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Community pharmacy New Medicines Service follow-up.

**Reason for letter:**  
To inform Aiden's GP of pharmacy NMS review findings, including poor inhaler technique requiring change of device and recommendation of formal asthma review.

**Background:**
- Diagnosed with asthma age 7
- New beclometasone 100 microgram metered-dose inhaler (with spacer) initiated by GP 8 weeks ago
- Concurrent salbutamol 100 microgram MDI PRN
- Previously stable on lower-dose budesonide via Easi-breathe device for 2 years; switch made due to NHS formulary alignment

**NMS interview today:**
- Aiden attended with father; engaged and articulate
- Father reports overuse of salbutamol (using PRN inhaler 3-4 times daily for past 4 weeks)
- Has used preventer beclometasone with spacer twice daily as prescribed
- 1 missed PE class in past 2 weeks due to wheeze
- No A&E attendances
- Inhaler technique check (with spacer): poor — Aiden uses spacer briefly but does not actuate first then breathe; uses single breath rather than tidal breathing
- Spacer not cleaned in past 6 weeks

**Action taken at pharmacy:**
- Demonstrated correct spacer technique with tidal breathing and counting
- Aiden practised technique and returned demonstration successfully
- Advised on weekly spacer cleaning (soapy water, air-dried)
- Reinforced importance of preventer therapy
- Provided BTS-format paediatric asthma action plan template
- Discussed importance of formal asthma review with the GP practice asthma nurse

**Current medications:**
- Beclometasone 100 microgram MDI, 2 puffs twice daily via spacer
- Salbutamol 100 microgram MDI PRN via spacer

**Past medical history:**
- Asthma (diagnosed age 7)
- Atopic eczema, well controlled
- Allergic rhinitis (seasonal)

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action requested from GP:**
- Formal asthma review with practice nurse within 2-4 weeks
- Consideration of step-up if symptoms persist despite improved technique
- Allergy trigger review (rhinitis season)
- Re-issue of asthma action plan signed by clinician

**Writing task:** Write a letter to Dr Sarah Ahmed at Riverside Medical Centre about today's NMS findings. Use letter prose. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Master Aiden Sullivan, aged 11, attended the pharmacy today with his father for a New Medicines Service follow-up of his recently switched beclometasone inhaler.
Beclometasone 100 micrograms twice daily via spacer was started by the GP eight weeks ago, replacing his previous budesonide device.
His father reports overuse of salbutamol at three to four times daily for the past four weeks.
Aiden has missed one physical education class in the past two weeks because of wheeze, with no A&E attendances.
Inhaler technique check revealed poor coordination, with the spacer used briefly and single-breath rather than tidal breathing.
The spacer has not been cleaned in the past six weeks.
Correct technique with tidal breathing has been demonstrated and Aiden practised the return demonstration successfully.
Weekly spacer cleaning advice has been provided.
A BTS-format paediatric asthma action plan template has been provided.
Past medical history includes asthma since age 7, well-controlled atopic eczema and seasonal allergic rhinitis.
Formal asthma review with the practice nurse within two to four weeks is requested.
Re-issue of a clinician-signed asthma action plan is requested.

scenarios.title: Response to specialist: tacrolimus level monitoring in transplant patient
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Eduardo Ribeiro  
**DOB:** 03/06/1975 (aged 50)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Hospital pharmacy, transplant pharmacy clinic.

**Reason for letter:**  
Response to Dr Helena Yong, Consultant Transplant Nephrologist (letter of 19/05/2026), regarding Mr Ribeiro's tacrolimus levels and concurrent diltiazem therapy following his renal transplant.

**Background from consultant letter (received 19/05/2026):**
- Renal transplant 14/02/2026 for diabetic nephropathy
- On tacrolimus (Prograf) 5 mg BD, mycophenolate mofetil 1 g BD, prednisolone 5 mg daily
- Recent ambulatory blood pressure measurement showed sustained hypertension; diltiazem 120 mg modified release daily was started two weeks ago
- Tacrolimus trough level 12 ng/mL (target 6-8) on 17/05/2026 (rising trend)
- Specialist requesting pharmacy review of dosing in context of diltiazem co-prescription

**Today's clinic findings:**
- Mr Ribeiro attended pharmacy clinic; well in himself
- No tremor, no headaches, no GI upset
- Renal function: creatinine 105 (baseline 95), eGFR 75 (baseline 85)
- Recent tacrolimus trough: 12 ng/mL (17/05); repeat today pending
- Blood pressure improved on diltiazem: 132/82
- Diabetes control: HbA1c 58 mmol/mol (acceptable post-transplant)
- LFTs normal

**Action taken in clinic:**
- Discussed CYP3A4 inhibition by diltiazem causing increased tacrolimus levels
- Tacrolimus dose reduced today from 5 mg BD to 3.5 mg BD
- Mycophenolate and prednisolone unchanged
- Repeat tacrolimus trough booked for 30/05/2026
- Detailed counselling on tacrolimus toxicity signs (tremor, headache, GI upset, raised creatinine)
- Advised to avoid grapefruit juice and any new medications without pharmacist consultation
- Plan: re-check level on 30/05; adjust dose to maintain trough 6-8 ng/mL

**Past medical history:**
- End-stage renal disease secondary to diabetic nephropathy (transplant 14/02/2026)
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus since 2008
- Hypertension
- Hypercholesterolaemia

**Allergies:** Penicillin (rash).

**Action requested from consultant:**
- Continue with tacrolimus dose change as documented
- Pharmacy will manage level monitoring and any further titration
- Communicate any cessation or modification of diltiazem in the future to allow pre-emptive tacrolimus dose adjustment
- Routine 3-month transplant clinic follow-up unchanged

**Writing task:** Write a reply letter to Dr Helena Yong summarising today's pharmacy clinic and the dose adjustment rationale. Use letter prose. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Eduardo Ribeiro, aged 50, attended the transplant pharmacy clinic today in response to Dr Yong's letter of 19 May 2026.
Following his renal transplant on 14 February 2026, he is on tacrolimus, mycophenolate mofetil and prednisolone immunosuppression.
Diltiazem 120 mg modified release daily was started two weeks ago for sustained hypertension; this is a known CYP3A4 inhibitor that increases tacrolimus levels.
His tacrolimus trough on 17 May 2026 was 12 ng/mL, above the target of 6 to 8.
Today he reports no tremor, headache or GI upset; creatinine has risen modestly from 95 to 105.
His blood pressure has improved on diltiazem to 132 over 82 mmHg.
Tacrolimus has been reduced today from 5 mg twice daily to 3.5 mg twice daily.
Mycophenolate mofetil and prednisolone are unchanged.
A repeat tacrolimus trough has been booked for 30 May 2026 with the plan to maintain a trough of 6 to 8 ng/mL.
He has been counselled on tacrolimus toxicity signs and advised to avoid grapefruit juice and any new medications without pharmacist consultation.
Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus since 2008, hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia.
He has a documented penicillin allergy, which caused a rash.
Pharmacy will continue to manage tacrolimus monitoring and titration.
The consultant is asked to communicate any future cessation or modification of diltiazem so that tacrolimus can be pre-emptively adjusted.

scenarios.title: Routine referral: continence assessment for early dementia
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Norman Whittaker  
**DOB:** 18/03/1942 (aged 84)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** District nursing service, home visit.

**Reason for referral:**  
Referral to the community continence service for assessment of urinary incontinence in the context of mild dementia and increasing carer burden.

**Background:**
- Mild dementia (Alzheimer's type) diagnosed 18 months ago
- Living at home with wife as primary carer
- Functional urinary incontinence (cannot reach toilet in time and forgets to go) emerging over past 3 months
- Daytime incontinence 2-3 episodes per day; nocturnal 1 per night
- No history of urinary tract infection

**Today's assessment by district nurse:**
- Mr Whittaker oriented to person; not to time or place
- Mobility good with walking stick
- Urinalysis: nitrites negative, leukocytes negative, protein trace
- Post-void residual scan: 80 mL (acceptable)
- Bladder diary kept by wife shows voids every 90 minutes
- Skin integrity intact, no pressure-area concerns
- Continence pads in use at night; reluctant to wear during day

**Carer assessment:**
- Wife (aged 79) is primary carer
- Reports fatigue from frequent toileting prompts and overnight changes
- Coping financially with continence pads via NHS supply
- Family (one daughter) visits weekly, lives 40 minutes away

**Current medications:**
- Donepezil 10 mg nocte
- Amlodipine 5 mg daily
- Atorvastatin 20 mg nocte
- No diuretics

**Past medical history:**
- Alzheimer's dementia (since 2024)
- Hypertension
- Hypercholesterolaemia
- Bilateral cataract surgery (2022)

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action taken today:**
- Bladder diary reviewed
- Continence pad supply confirmed via prescription
- Reinforced 2-hourly toileting prompts
- Discussed bladder retraining strategies appropriate for cognitive level
- Provided carer support information for wife

**Action requested from continence service:**
- Specialist continence assessment
- Consideration of timed voiding programme
- Equipment recommendations (raised toilet seat, commode for night use)
- Carer-specific advice for dementia-related incontinence

**Writing task:** Write a routine referral letter to Specialist Continence Nurse Ms Janet Chowdhury, City Community Continence Service, requesting assessment. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Norman Whittaker, aged 84, has emerging functional urinary incontinence in the context of mild Alzheimer's dementia.
Dementia was diagnosed 18 months ago; his cognition is sufficient for orientation to person but not to time or place.
Incontinence has developed over the past three months with two to three daytime and one nocturnal episode.
Urinalysis today was negative for infection and post-void residual scan was 80 mL.
His skin integrity is intact and he uses continence pads at night.
His wife, aged 79, is the primary carer and reports fatigue from frequent toileting prompts and overnight changes.
Past medical history includes Alzheimer's dementia since 2024, hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia.
He is not on any diuretic medication.
Two-hourly toileting prompts have been reinforced and bladder retraining strategies appropriate to his cognitive level have been discussed.
Specialist continence assessment is requested.
Consideration of a timed voiding programme is suggested.
Equipment recommendations including a raised toilet seat and night commode would be welcome.

scenarios.title: Discharge to GP: post-falls inpatient stay with rehabilitation
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Florence Akande  
**DOB:** 19/05/1939 (aged 87)  
**Admission date:** 14/05/2026  
**Discharge date:** 26/05/2026 (today)

**Reason for admission:**  
Mechanical fall at home with hip fracture; admitted for surgical repair and rehabilitation.

**Admission summary:**
- Sustained left intracapsular neck of femur fracture after fall in living room
- Surgical hemi-arthroplasty performed 15/05/2026 by Mr K Chen (orthopaedic registrar)
- Spinal anaesthesia; no intra-operative complications
- 12-day inpatient stay for surgery and rehabilitation

**Inpatient progress:**
- Day 1 post-op: physiotherapy commenced, weight-bearing as tolerated
- Day 3: ambulating with Zimmer frame on the ward
- Day 7: progressed to walking stick
- Day 10: independent transfers; stair assessment passed
- Pain controlled with paracetamol regularly plus codeine PRN (used twice in first 3 days only)
- Surgical wound healed well; clips removed day 10
- Tinzaparin 4500 units subcutaneously daily for VTE prophylaxis, completed 14-day course
- Bone health review: started on alendronic acid 70 mg weekly plus vitamin D 800 units daily
- No new pressure areas; continent of urine and faeces with prompting

**At discharge:**
- Mobile with walking stick at baseline level
- Wound healed, no infection
- Self-caring with personal hygiene
- Cognition stable; no inpatient confusion
- Returning home with daughter who has taken 2 weeks' compassionate leave to support recovery
- Falls risk: enhanced; package of care arranged for ongoing OT/physio in community

**Past medical history:**
- Osteoporosis (T-score -2.8 lumbar spine on DEXA in 2023; not previously on treatment)
- Hypertension, on amlodipine 5 mg daily
- Mild cognitive impairment (no formal dementia diagnosis)
- Recurrent falls (3 in past 12 months)

**Allergies:** Penicillin (rash, childhood).

**Discharge medications:**
- Paracetamol 1 g QDS regular
- Codeine 30 mg QDS PRN (max 3 days)
- Lactulose 15 mL BD PRN (codeine-related)
- Alendronic acid 70 mg weekly (Mondays)
- Calcium and vitamin D combined tablet two daily
- Amlodipine 5 mg daily (continued)

**Home circumstances:**
- Lives alone in two-bedroom house; daughter living with her for 2 weeks
- One step at front door; handrail in place
- Has pendant alarm
- Community OT home visit arranged for week 1 post-discharge for risk assessment

**Required follow-up:**
- GP review at 4 weeks for medication review and bone health
- Orthopaedic follow-up at 6 weeks (already booked)
- Community physiotherapy ongoing for 6 weeks
- Falls clinic referral made to commence in 4 weeks

**Writing task:** Write a discharge letter to Dr Sarah Ahmed, Mrs Akande's GP at Riverside Medical Centre. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mrs Florence Akande was admitted on 14 May 2026 with a left intracapsular neck of femur fracture sustained in a mechanical fall at home.
She underwent left hemi-arthroplasty on 15 May 2026 under spinal anaesthesia, with no intra-operative complications.
Her post-operative course has been uncomplicated; she has progressed through physiotherapy from Zimmer frame to walking stick.
She is independently transferring and has passed stair assessment at the time of discharge.
Tinzaparin prophylaxis has been completed and her surgical wound has healed with clips removed on day 10.
Bone health has been reviewed and she has been commenced on alendronic acid 70 mg weekly and combined calcium and vitamin D.
Past medical history includes osteoporosis, hypertension on amlodipine and mild cognitive impairment with recurrent falls.
She has a documented penicillin allergy, which caused a childhood rash.
She lives alone but her daughter is taking two weeks' compassionate leave to support recovery at home.
Community physiotherapy and an occupational therapy home assessment have been arranged.
Orthopaedic follow-up is booked at six weeks and a falls clinic referral has been made.
GP review at four weeks is requested for medication review and bone health follow-up.

scenarios.title: Response to GP: school nurse asthma plan implementation feedback

case_notes_structured.sentence: Master James Becker, aged 12, has had his school-based asthma action plan implemented over the past six weeks in response to Dr Khan's letter of 19 May 2026.

scenarios.title: Urgent referral: deteriorating wound with sepsis features
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Ngozi Okafor  
**DOB:** 22/08/1957 (aged 68)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026, 11:30 hrs  
**Setting:** District nursing visit at patient's home.

**Reason for urgent referral:**  
Deterioration of pre-existing diabetic foot ulcer with new systemic features of infection, requiring same-day medical assessment.

**Wound background:**
- Right plantar diabetic foot ulcer, present for 6 months
- Previously managed with weekly district nurse dressings using hydrofibre and foam, with offloading boot
- Slow but steady improvement until 3 weeks ago

**Today's findings during routine visit:**
- Wound has increased in size from 2 by 2 cm to 4 by 3 cm over past 2 weeks
- New offensive odour
- Surrounding skin: extensive erythema (3 cm beyond wound margin)
- Increased exudate, now purulent
- Patient reports new throbbing pain rated 7/10 (previously pain-free)
- Visible exposed bone at base of wound (probe-to-bone test positive)

**Systemic features:**
- Temperature 38.1 degrees Celsius
- Pulse 108 regular
- BP 116/72
- Respiratory rate 22
- SpO2 96% on room air
- Capillary blood glucose 18.4 mmol/L
- Patient reports feeling generally unwell, fatigued, reduced appetite past 24 hours

**Past medical history:**
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (since 2010), on metformin 1 g BD and gliclazide 80 mg BD; HbA1c 78 mmol/mol last month
- Diabetic peripheral neuropathy
- Hypertension, on ramipril 10 mg daily
- Mild chronic kidney disease (eGFR 52)

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action taken today:**
- Vital signs documented and patient informed
- Wound photographed for handover
- Wound dressing changed with non-adherent gauze (definitive dressing deferred for medical review)
- Telephoned GP same-day; GP unable to attend; advised same-day A&E attendance
- Ambulance booked for transfer to Royal Northern Hospital A&E
- Family member contacted (daughter to meet patient at hospital)

**Home circumstances:**
- Lives alone in ground-floor flat
- Daughter lives 30 minutes away
- Mobile with crutches due to offloading boot

**Action requested from on-call medical/surgical team:**
- Urgent clinical assessment with sepsis screening
- Wound swab and consideration of imaging for osteomyelitis
- Glycaemic stabilisation
- Admission likely for IV antibiotics and surgical opinion

**Writing task:** Write an urgent referral letter to the on-call medical/surgical team at Royal Northern Hospital A&E, requesting same-day assessment. Use letter prose. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mrs Ngozi Okafor, aged 68, presents today with a deteriorating diabetic foot ulcer and new systemic features of infection requiring urgent medical assessment.
Her right plantar diabetic foot ulcer has been present for six months, previously managed by community nursing with weekly dressings and offloading boot.
Over the past two weeks the wound has enlarged from 2 by 2 cm to 4 by 3 cm with new offensive odour, extensive surrounding erythema and purulent exudate.
Visible bone is exposed at the base of the wound and probe-to-bone test is positive, suggesting possible osteomyelitis.
She has new throbbing pain rated 7 out of 10, replacing her previous pain-free state.
Systemic features today include temperature 38.1 degrees Celsius, pulse 108, blood pressure 116 over 72, respiratory rate 22 and oxygen saturations 96 percent on room air.
Her capillary blood glucose today is 18.4 mmol/L and she reports general malaise and reduced appetite over the past 24 hours.
Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes since 2010 (HbA1c 78 last month), diabetic peripheral neuropathy, hypertension and mild chronic kidney disease.
Current medications include metformin 1 g twice daily, gliclazide 80 mg twice daily, ramipril 10 mg daily and her usual dressings.
The wound has been photographed for handover and dressed with non-adherent gauze, with definitive dressing deferred for medical review.
An ambulance has been booked for transfer and her daughter has been contacted to meet her at hospital.
Urgent sepsis screening, wound swab, consideration of imaging for osteomyelitis and glycaemic stabilisation are requested.

scenarios.title: Transfer to specialist: palliative care handover
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:

**Setting:** Acute oncology ward to specialist palliative care unit.

**Reason for transfer:**  
Deteriorating end-stage metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma. Symptom burden is now beyond what can be managed on the oncology ward; transfer to specialist palliative care unit at St Mary's Hospice for symptom optimisation and end-of-life care planning.

**Disease background:**
- Pancreatic adenocarcinoma diagnosed November 2025
- Metastatic disease to liver and lung at diagnosis
- Completed 4 cycles palliative FOLFIRINOX; discontinued February 2026 due to poor tolerance
- Best supportive care since February 2026

**Current admission:**
- Admitted 21/05/2026 with severe abdominal pain, nausea, and reduced oral intake
- Performance status declined from ECOG 2 to ECOG 3 during admission
- Patient and family have had advance care planning conversations with oncology team
- Documented wish: care in hospice setting; do not attempt cardiopulmonary resuscitation
- DNACPR form completed 23/05/2026

**Current symptom burden:**
- Pain: continuous epigastric pain radiating to back, 7/10 at rest, 9/10 on movement
- Nausea: persistent despite ondansetron and cyclizine
- Constipation: last bowel movement 4 days ago
- Anorexia: minimal oral intake; nausea triggered by food
- Anxiety: episodes of acute distress, particularly at night
- Fatigue: bedbound for last 48 hours

**Current medications:**
- Morphine sulphate modified release 60 mg BD
- Morphine sulphate immediate release 10 mg PRN (using 4-6 doses/24 hours)
- Cyclizine 50 mg TDS
- Ondansetron 8 mg BD
- Movicol 1 sachet BD
- Lorazepam 0.5 mg PRN
- Dexamethasone 4 mg morning

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Social situation:**
- Lives with wife (primary carer, supportive)
- Two adult children, regular visitors
- Family aware of prognosis and supportive of hospice transfer

**Action requested from palliative care team:**
- Optimisation of pain management; consider syringe driver if oral route unreliable
- Anti-emetic review (haloperidol or methotrimeprazine consideration)
- Bowel regimen review
- Anxiety management; psychological and family support
- Care of the dying pathway initiation when appropriate
- Family bereavement support coordination

**Writing task:** Write a transfer letter to the senior nurse at St Mary's Hospice palliative care unit summarising Mr Janssen's situation and outstanding care needs. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Walter Janssen, aged 70, is being transferred to St Mary's Hospice for specialist palliative care.

scenarios.title: Discharge to GP: brief admission with acute gastritis
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Patrick O'Connell  
**DOB:** 17/01/1962 (aged 64)  
**Admission date:** 24/05/2026  
**Discharge date:** 26/05/2026 (today)

**Reason for admission:**  
Acute epigastric pain with melaena and NSAID use; admitted for endoscopy and observation.

**Presenting complaint on admission:**
- 48-hour history of severe burning epigastric pain
- One episode of melaena overnight
- No haematemesis; no syncope
- Self-purchased ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily for last 10 days for shoulder pain

**Investigations on admission:**
- Haemoglobin 124 g/L (baseline 138); MCV 88
- Urea 9.8 (raised), creatinine 84
- Clotting normal
- Upright chest X-ray: no free air
- Upper GI endoscopy (24/05/2026): erosive antral gastritis, no active bleeding, Forrest III (clean base) duodenal ulcer
- Rapid urease test: positive (Helicobacter pylori)

**Inpatient management:**
- Intravenous pantoprazole 80 mg bolus then 8 mg/hour infusion for 24 hours
- Switched to oral pantoprazole 40 mg twice daily on day 2
- Ibuprofen discontinued; paracetamol substituted
- IV fluids on day 1; observation of haemodynamic stability

**Progress:**
- No further melaena since admission
- Haemodynamically stable throughout
- Haemoglobin stable at 122 on day 3
- Tolerating diet; mobile independently

**At discharge:**
- Discharge medications: oral pantoprazole 40 mg twice daily for two weeks then 20 mg daily for further six weeks; clarithromycin 500 mg twice daily and amoxicillin 1 g twice daily for seven days (H. pylori eradication)
- Patient counselled to avoid NSAIDs lifelong
- Patient counselled on alarm symptoms to return to A&E

**Past medical history:**
- Right shoulder rotator cuff tendinopathy (long-standing)
- Hypertension, on amlodipine 5 mg daily

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Follow-up plan:**
- GP to arrange urea breath test in 12 weeks to confirm eradication (omitting PPI for 14 days prior)
- GP to consider alternative analgesia for shoulder symptoms

**Writing task:** Write a discharge letter to Dr Imran Khan, GP at The Meadows Surgery, requesting ongoing care. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mr Patrick O'Connell was admitted on 24 May 2026 with acute epigastric pain and one episode of melaena.
He had been taking self-purchased ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily for ten days for shoulder pain.
Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy revealed erosive antral gastritis and a Forrest III duodenal ulcer.
Rapid urease test was positive for Helicobacter pylori.
Haemoglobin fell from a baseline of 138 to 124 g/L on admission and remained stable at 122 g/L by day 3.
He was treated with intravenous then oral pantoprazole and ibuprofen was discontinued.
He remained haemodynamically stable with no further melaena throughout the admission.
Discharge medications include oral pantoprazole, clarithromycin and amoxicillin for Helicobacter pylori eradication.
Past medical history includes hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily and rotator cuff tendinopathy.
He has been counselled to avoid non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs lifelong.
GP urea breath test in twelve weeks is requested, withholding the proton pump inhibitor for fourteen days prior.
GP consideration of alternative analgesia for ongoing shoulder symptoms is requested.

scenarios.title: Pharmacy communication: new oral contraceptive interaction with lamotrigine
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Miss Eleanor Pritchard  
**DOB:** 30/06/2003 (aged 22)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Community pharmacy clinical check.

**Reason for letter:**  
New prescription for combined oral contraceptive pill (Microgynon 30) collected today by patient already on lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily for epilepsy. Significant bidirectional interaction requires GP review.

**Today's pharmacy encounter:**
- Patient attended to collect new prescription for Microgynon 30, prescribed by trainee GP on 24 May 2026
- Patient currently established on lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily for focal epilepsy
- Last seizure: 14 months ago
- Driving licence current

**Interaction identified:**
- Combined oral contraceptives induce glucuronidation of lamotrigine, reducing plasma concentrations by approximately 50 percent during pill-taking days
- Conversely, lamotrigine levels rise during the pill-free week (risk of toxicity)
- Bidirectional interaction may compromise both seizure control and contraceptive efficacy

**Patient discussion:**
- Patient was unaware of interaction
- Patient counselled briefly about the clinical concern
- Microgynon withheld pending GP review
- Patient understood and consented to withhold
- Patient counselled on importance of contraception planning, not stopping suddenly

**Action requested from GP:**
- Review choice of contraception in light of the interaction
- Consider progestogen-only contraception (mini-pill, depot, implant or IUD) as alternatives without significant lamotrigine interaction
- If combined oral contraceptive deemed essential, consider lamotrigine dose-monitoring strategy
- Confirm action plan with patient

**Past medical history (records):**
- Focal epilepsy since 2018, on lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily, well controlled
- No other medications

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Writing task:** Write a communication letter to Dr Akinwale Adeyemi, GP at Riverside Medical Centre, alerting to the interaction and requesting review of the contraceptive choice. Use letter prose. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Miss Eleanor Pritchard, aged 22, attended the pharmacy today to collect a new prescription for Microgynon 30.
She is established on lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily for focal epilepsy with her last seizure 14 months ago.
A bidirectional interaction between combined oral contraceptives and lamotrigine has been identified at clinical check.
Combined oral contraceptives reduce lamotrigine plasma concentrations by approximately 50 percent during pill-taking days.
Lamotrigine levels conversely rise during the pill-free week, with risk of toxicity.
The interaction may compromise both seizure control and contraceptive efficacy.
The patient was previously unaware of the interaction and has consented to withholding Microgynon pending GP review.
Progestogen-only contraceptive alternatives (mini-pill, depot, implant or intrauterine device) are without significant lamotrigine interaction.
Her driving licence is current and she has been counselled not to stop her contraception suddenly.
She has no other regular medications and no known drug allergies.
Confirmation of an agreed action plan with the patient is requested.
Past medical history is limited to her stable epilepsy diagnosis since 2018.

scenarios.title: Pharmacy response: smoking cessation pharmacotherapy advice
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mr Karl Schoeman

**Setting:** Pharmacy-led smoking-cessation clinic.

**Reason for letter:**  
Response to GP enquiry of 19 May 2026 about smoking-cessation pharmacotherapy options for Mr Schoeman after one failed attempt with nicotine replacement therapy.

**GP enquiry:**
- Mr Schoeman wishes to make a further quit attempt before scheduled bariatric surgery in three months
- Previous attempt with nicotine replacement patches plus oral spray (combination therapy) achieved 4 weeks of abstinence before relapse, eight months ago
- GP requests pharmacist input on next-line pharmacotherapy options

**Today's clinic assessment:**
- Currently smoking 18 cigarettes per day for 28 years
- Fagerstrom Test for Nicotine Dependence: 7 (high dependence)
- Quit date set: 16 June 2026 (3 weeks from today)
- Motivation: 8/10
- Withdrawal symptoms previously included irritability, sleep disturbance, food cravings
- No history of seizures, no psychiatric illness, no severe renal impairment
- Carbon monoxide reading today: 24 ppm (smoker range)

**Past medical history:**
- Obesity (BMI 42), awaiting bariatric surgery
- Hypertension, on amlodipine 5 mg daily
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus, on metformin 1 g twice daily and dapagliflozin 10 mg daily
- Mild hypercholesterolaemia, on atorvastatin 20 mg nightly
- No psychiatric history

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Pharmacy plan agreed:**
- Commence varenicline 0.5 mg daily for 3 days, increase to 0.5 mg twice daily for 4 days, then 1 mg twice daily from day 8 of treatment
- Start 8 days before quit date (8 June 2026)
- Total course 12 weeks
- Behavioural support: weekly pharmacy appointments for 4 weeks, then fortnightly for 8 weeks
- Patient counselled on common side effects: nausea (most common, dose-related), sleep disturbance, vivid dreams
- Patient warned about rare neuropsychiatric side effects with safety-netting advice
- Patient counselled to stop smoking from day 8 of varenicline
- Follow-up CO reading at week 4 and week 12

**Writing task:** Write a response letter to Dr Adeyemi, GP, summarising the assessment, plan agreed and pharmacy follow-up arrangements. Write between 180 and 200 words.

scenarios.title: Pharmacy communication: high-dose proton pump inhibitor and overdose risk
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Marjorie Pearson  
**DOB:** 02/02/1939 (aged 87)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Community pharmacy medicines use review (MUR) clinic visit.

Duplication of proton pump inhibitor (PPI) therapy identified during MUR, with cumulative dose far exceeding recommended geriatric maximum and likely contributing to recurrent falls and hyponatraemia.

**Today's MUR findings:**
- Patient brought her medication bag
- Two PPIs identified concurrently: omeprazole 20 mg twice daily AND lansoprazole 30 mg once daily
- Total daily PPI exposure equivalent to approximately 70 mg omeprazole daily — far above recommended geriatric maximum (20 mg daily)
- Patient unaware she was on two PPIs; cannot explain how each was started
- Both PPIs on current repeat prescription

**Recent clinical concerns (from patient's account):**
- Three falls in the past four months; one resulted in left hip bruising and a 4-day hospital observation in March 2026
- Hospital noted hyponatraemia (sodium 128 mmol/L) on March admission
- General fatigue and 'wobbly' feeling on standing
- Last documented bone density scan: T-score -2.6 (osteoporosis) in 2023
- On alendronate 70 mg weekly since 2023

**Other current medications:**
- Omeprazole 20 mg twice daily
- Lansoprazole 30 mg daily
- Amlodipine 10 mg daily
- Bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily
- Apixaban 5 mg twice daily (for atrial fibrillation)
- Alendronate 70 mg weekly
- Adcal-D3 1 tablet daily
- Sertraline 50 mg daily

**Past medical history:**
- Atrial fibrillation
- Hypertension
- Osteoporosis (with prior wrist fracture)
- Mild cognitive impairment (independent for ADLs)
- Recurrent falls
- Hyponatraemia (under investigation)

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Pharmacy actions today:**
- One PPI temporarily withheld; lansoprazole continued, omeprazole withheld pending GP review (patient consented)
- Patient counselled on hyponatraemia and falls link with high-dose PPI
- Patient counselled on potential need for sodium monitoring if dose reduced
- 14-day supply omeprazole withheld; patient understands rationale

**Writing task:** Write a communication letter to Dr Akinwale Adeyemi, GP at Riverside Medical Centre, alerting to the PPI duplication, the likely clinical impact and the action taken at the pharmacy. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mrs Marjorie Pearson, aged 87, attended the pharmacy today for a medicines use review.

She has had three falls in the past four months and a 4-day hospital observation in March 2026.
Hyponatraemia with sodium of 128 mmol/L was noted during the March 2026 admission.
She is on apixaban 5 mg twice daily for atrial fibrillation and alendronate 70 mg weekly for osteoporosis.
She has past medical history of atrial fibrillation, hypertension, osteoporosis with prior wrist fracture and mild cognitive impairment.
Omeprazole has been withheld today with patient consent and lansoprazole continued.
She has been counselled on the link between high-dose PPI therapy, hyponatraemia and falls risk.
She has been counselled on the need for sodium monitoring if dose is reduced.

scenarios.title: Nursing communication: school nursing letter regarding asthma plan
