**Reason for letter:**  
Communication of updated asthma management plan to school nurse following step-up in inhaled corticosteroid dose.

**Asthma background:**
- Mild persistent asthma diagnosed 2021
- Currently on Clenil 100 micrograms beclomethasone two puffs twice daily and salbutamol PRN
- Three exacerbations in past 6 months requiring oral prednisolone bursts; one resulted in 24-hour hospital observation in February 2026
- Generally well controlled between exacerbations
- Confident with inhaler use via spacer

**Today's review findings:**
- Mother and patient attended clinic
- Peak flow today: 220 L/min (predicted 250 L/min)
- Asthma Control Test: 17 (poorly controlled)
- Inhaler technique reviewed and good with spacer
- Recent classroom symptoms in PE lessons noted
- Spacer device at school confirmed; spare salbutamol inhaler held in school office

**Updated management plan:**
- Step up to Clenil 250 micrograms beclomethasone two puffs twice daily
- Pre-exercise salbutamol two puffs 10 minutes before PE
- New personalised asthma action plan issued
- Annual review at allergy clinic for atopy assessment

**Past medical history:**
- Mild atopic eczema, controlled with emollients
- Allergic rhinitis (seasonal), on cetirizine PRN

**Allergies:** Nil known to medications.

**Action requested from school nurse:**
- Distribution of updated asthma action plan to class teacher and PE teacher
- Confirmation of in-date spare salbutamol inhaler and spacer at school
- Reinforcement with PE teacher about pre-exercise salbutamol
- Annual asthma training for relevant staff (offered through clinic outreach)
- Communication with parent if any concerns about school-based asthma symptoms

**Writing task:** Write a communication letter to School Nurse Mrs Caroline Westwood at Holly Lane Primary School about Sophia's updated asthma plan. Use letter prose. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Miss Sophia Lin, aged 10, has mild persistent asthma diagnosed in 2021 and attended the nurse-led asthma clinic today.
She has had three exacerbations in the past six months requiring oral prednisolone bursts.
One exacerbation in February 2026 required 24-hour hospital observation.
Her Asthma Control Test today was 17, indicating poor control, with peak flow of 220 L/min against a predicted 250 L/min.
Inhaler technique with a spacer was reviewed and is good.
Recent classroom symptoms during PE lessons have been noted.
Inhaled corticosteroid has been stepped up from beclomethasone 100 micrograms to 250 micrograms two puffs twice daily.
Pre-exercise salbutamol two puffs 10 minutes before PE has been added to the plan.
An updated personalised asthma action plan has been issued.
A spare salbutamol inhaler and spacer device are held in the school office.
Past medical history includes mild atopic eczema and seasonal allergic rhinitis on cetirizine as required.
An annual review at the allergy clinic for atopy assessment is planned.

scenarios.title: Nursing discharge: post-cataract day-case ophthalmology
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Doris Lyle  
**DOB:** 12/09/1939 (aged 86)  
**Day-case admission:** 26/05/2026 (today)  
**Setting:** Day-case ophthalmology surgery unit, City General Hospital.

**Reason for admission:**  
Elective right cataract phacoemulsification with intra-ocular lens implantation under local anaesthesia.

**Pre-operative findings:**
- Right eye: dense nuclear cataract, visual acuity 6/24
- Left eye: pseudophakia (operated 2024), visual acuity 6/6
- Otherwise no ophthalmic concerns; intra-ocular pressures normal
- Pre-assessment clinic appointment 12 May 2026; suitable for day case

**Procedure:**
- Topical anaesthesia
- Routine phacoemulsification with foldable IOL
- No intra-operative complications; total operative time 18 minutes

**Post-operative course (today):**
- Right eye padded for 4 hours then removed
- Comfortable; mild gritty sensation only
- Visual acuity through pinhole at 2 hours: 6/12 right (improvement expected over 4-6 weeks)
- Observations stable throughout day-case stay
- Tolerated oral fluids and light meal
- Mobilised independently; collected by daughter

**Discharge medications:**
- Chloramphenicol 0.5 percent eye drops one drop four times daily for 7 days
- Dexamethasone 0.1 percent eye drops one drop four times daily for 28 days, reducing

**Post-operative advice:**
- Avoid bending below the waist, lifting heavy objects or strenuous activity for 1 week
- No swimming for 4 weeks
- Clear plastic eye shield at night for 1 week
- Alarm signs: increasing pain, vision loss, redness, photophobia — emergency contact provided

**Past medical history:**
- Hypertension, on amlodipine 5 mg daily
- Mild osteoarthritis of the hands
- Bilateral cataracts (right operated today, left previously operated)

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Follow-up:**
- Routine post-operative check in optometry community service in 2 weeks
- GP review not routinely required unless concerns

**Writing task:** Write a discharge letter to Mrs Lyle's GP, Dr Imran Khan at The Meadows Surgery, informing of the day-case procedure and post-operative plan, and providing any information the GP needs (e.g., new drops, safety-netting). Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mrs Doris Lyle was admitted as a day case today for elective right cataract phacoemulsification under local anaesthesia.
The procedure was uncomplicated with a foldable intra-ocular lens implanted; total operative time was 18 minutes.
Post-operatively she has been comfortable with only mild gritty sensation.
Right visual acuity through pinhole at two hours post-operatively was 6 over 12, with further improvement expected over four to six weeks.
She has been discharged on chloramphenicol 0.5 percent eye drops four times daily for seven days.
She has also been discharged on dexamethasone 0.1 percent eye drops four times daily for 28 days, on a reducing schedule.
She has been advised to wear a clear plastic eye shield at night for one week.
She has been advised to avoid bending, lifting and strenuous activity for one week and swimming for four weeks.
Safety-netting advice for increasing pain, vision loss, redness or photophobia has been given.
Routine post-operative review will be undertaken by the community optometry service in two weeks.
Past medical history includes hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily and previous left cataract surgery in 2024.
She mobilised independently and was collected by her daughter on discharge.

scenarios.title: Nursing referral: community mental health team for low mood post-bereavement
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Rosemary Cartwright  
**DOB:** 11/05/1944 (aged 81)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** District nursing visit (long-term condition follow-up turned mental-health concern).

**Reason for referral:**  
Deteriorating mood, sleep, appetite and engagement four months following bereavement of husband; new passive death wish disclosed today.

**Today's encounter (district nursing visit):**
- Routine 6-weekly leg ulcer dressing visit
- Patient appears withdrawn, tearful and unkempt (uncharacteristic)
- Reports: 'I just want to be with him'; on direct enquiry, denies active plans to end her own life
- No active self-harm; no recent substance use
- Has stopped taking pleasure in usual activities (gardening, family contact)
- Sleeps 3-4 hours per night with early morning waking
- Appetite reduced; lost approximately 4 kg since husband's death four months ago
- Has not left the house apart from medical visits
- No insight initially but engaged in discussion today

**Bereavement background:**
- Husband of 58 years died 24 January 2026 following short illness
- Couple had no children; her sister (74) lives 25 miles away and visits monthly
- Was previously well; never accessed mental health services

**Past medical history:**
- Bilateral venous leg ulcers (right healed; left healing well)
- Hypertension, on amlodipine 5 mg daily
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus, on metformin 500 mg twice daily (HbA1c 51)
- No previous psychiatric history

**Current medications:**
- Amlodipine 5 mg daily
- Metformin 500 mg twice daily
- Paracetamol PRN

**Risk assessment today:**
- Risk to self: passive death wish; no active plan; access to medications limited
- Risk to others: nil
- Risk of self-neglect: significant (weight loss, sleep, social withdrawal)
- Living circumstance: lives alone; bungalow; no immediate community alarm in place

**Action taken today:**
- Sister contacted with patient's consent; sister will visit tomorrow
- Patient declined same-day GP visit but consented to GP being contacted
- District nurse staying for full hour; verbal safety contract until tomorrow
- Care of Yourself leaflet provided
- Discussed bereavement counselling options

**Writing task:** Write a routine referral letter to Dr Helen Lim, Consultant Psychiatrist (Older People), Riverside Community Mental Health Team, requesting psychiatric assessment for new-onset moderate depression in the context of bereavement, with safety concerns short of immediate risk. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mrs Rosemary Cartwright, aged 81, was visited by the district nurse today for routine leg ulcer care.
Her husband of 58 years died on 24 January 2026, four months ago.
She presented as withdrawn, tearful and unkempt, which is uncharacteristic.
She disclosed a passive death wish ('I just want to be with him') but denied active plans to end her own life.
She sleeps only three to four hours per night with early morning waking.
Appetite is reduced and she has lost approximately 4 kg since her husband's death.
She has stopped taking pleasure in gardening and family contact.
She has not left the house except for medical visits since the bereavement.
She has no previous psychiatric history.
Her sister, who lives 25 miles away, has been contacted with consent and will visit tomorrow.
Past medical history includes bilateral venous leg ulcers, hypertension and well-controlled type 2 diabetes.
A verbal safety contract has been agreed with the patient until tomorrow.

scenarios.title: Nursing referral: community midwifery — gestational diabetes for dietitian input
scenarios.case_notes_markdown:
**Patient:** Mrs Beatrice Anand  
**DOB:** 19/03/1991 (aged 34)  
**Today's date:** 26/05/2026  
**Setting:** Community midwifery review, antenatal clinic.

**Reason for referral:**  
Newly diagnosed gestational diabetes mellitus at 28 weeks' gestation, requesting dietitian input prior to insulin consideration.

**Current pregnancy:**
- Gravida 2, Para 1
- Current gestation: 28+3 weeks
- Singleton pregnancy; anomaly scan 20 weeks normal
- Growth scan today: estimated fetal weight on 78th centile (asymmetric; abdominal circumference on 92nd centile)
- No signs of pre-eclampsia; BP 124/76 today

**Glycaemic findings:**
- 75 g oral glucose tolerance test on 19 May 2026:
  - Fasting glucose 5.8 mmol/L
  - 2-hour post-load glucose 9.4 mmol/L
  - Diagnostic of gestational diabetes (NICE NG3 thresholds: fasting >= 5.6 or 2-hour >= 7.8)
- HbA1c at 12 weeks gestation: 38 mmol/mol (not diagnostic of pre-existing diabetes)
- Home glucose monitoring commenced today

**Risk factors:**
- BMI at booking: 31
- First-degree family history: mother type 2 diabetes
- Previous baby birth weight 4.2 kg in 2022
- South Asian ethnicity

**Past obstetric history:**
- 2022: normal vaginal delivery at term; baby 4.2 kg; no gestational diabetes screened at the time

**Past medical history:**
- Iron-deficiency anaemia (resolved with oral iron in last pregnancy)
- No other chronic conditions

**Current medications:**
- Pregnacare antenatal multivitamin
- Ferrous sulphate 200 mg daily (since 22 weeks for borderline iron stores)

**Allergies:** Nil known.

**Action taken today:**
- Diagnosis explained
- Home blood glucose meter and supplies issued
- Diary commenced — fasting and 1-hour post-meal targets explained (NICE NG3: fasting < 5.3 mmol/L; 1-hour post-meal < 7.8 mmol/L)
- Patient counselled on dietary measures
- Joint antenatal/diabetic clinic appointment booked for 2 June 2026
- Growth scans planned every 4 weeks

**Writing task:** Write a routine referral letter to Mrs Sarah Patel, Antenatal Dietitian at City Maternity Unit, requesting dietitian input for newly diagnosed gestational diabetes prior to consideration of pharmacotherapy. Write between 180 and 200 words.

case_notes_structured.sentence: Mrs Beatrice Anand, aged 34, has been newly diagnosed with gestational diabetes mellitus at 28 weeks' gestation in her second pregnancy.
Her 75 g oral glucose tolerance test on 19 May 2026 showed fasting glucose of 5.8 mmol/L and 2-hour post-load glucose of 9.4 mmol/L.
Booking HbA1c was 38 mmol/mol, excluding pre-existing diabetes.
Risk factors include BMI of 31 at booking, family history of type 2 diabetes and South Asian ethnicity.
Her previous baby in 2022 weighed 4.2 kg at term, unscreened for gestational diabetes at the time.
Growth scan today showed estimated fetal weight on the 78th centile with abdominal circumference on the 92nd centile.
Blood pressure was 124 over 76 mmHg with no signs of pre-eclampsia.
Home glucose monitoring has been commenced today and a glucose meter has been issued.
She has been counselled on dietary measures and NICE NG3 glycaemic targets.
A joint antenatal/diabetic clinic appointment has been booked for 2 June 2026.
She is currently on Pregnacare antenatal multivitamin and ferrous sulphate 200 mg daily.
Growth scans are planned every four weeks for the remainder of the pregnancy.
